Furthermore, the fact that the principal perpetrator, Sam, was not convicted does not preclude the companions from being tried as accomplices, as their liability is determined by their own actions in relation to the crime.

Answer: (C)

evidence_store.text: Finally, an individual may be held criminally liable for the acts of another, including first-degree murder, as an accomplice. In order to sustain a conviction based on accomplice liability, the Commonwealth must demonstrate that an individual acted with the intent of promoting or facilitating the commission of an offense and agrees, aids, or attempts to aid such other person in either planning or committing that offense. Commonwealth v. Spotz , 552 Pa. 499, 716 A.2d 580, 585-86 (1998). As with conspiracy, a shared criminal intent between the principal and his accomplice may be inferred from a defendant's words or conduct or from the attendant circumstances. Id.

To be convicted as an accomplice under the prevailing rule, a person must have given aid, counsel, or encouragement with the intent to aid or encourage the principal in the commission of the crime charged. Mere knowledge that a crime would result from the aid provided is generally insufficient for accomplice liability.

Thus, to establish accomplice liability, the State must prove that: (1) the accomplice had the purpose to make the crime succeed; (2) the accomplice's acts solicited, aided, or attempted to aid another in committing the offense; and (3) under paragraph IV, the accomplice shared the requisite mental state for the offense. See State v. Winward, 161 N.H. 533, 543, 20 A.3d 338 (2011).

[9] The State alleged that Parsley committed his offenses in conjunction with Ashlee and Joseph, and the trial court instructed the jury on accomplice liability. "A person who knowingly or intentionally aids, induces, or causes another person to commit an offense commits that offense ...." Ind. Code § 35-41-2-4 (1977). We consider the following factors in determining whether a defendant aided another in the commission of a crime: (1) presence at the scene of the crime; (2) companionship with another engaged in a crime; (3) failure to oppose the commission of the crime; and (4) the course of conduct before, during, and after the occurrence of the crime. Wieland v. State , 736 N.E.2d 1198, 1202 (Ind. 2000). Accomplice liability applies to the contemplated offense and to all acts that are a probable and natural consequence of the concerted action. Id. It is not necessary for the jury to find that a defendant participated in every element of the crime. Vitek v. State , 750 N.E.2d 346, 352 (Ind. 2001). Further, an accomplice may be convicted of aiding another person in committing an offense even if the other person is acquitted of the offense. Ind. Code § 35-41-2-4.

Defendant was found guilty of assault in the first degree. See § 565.050. A person commits this crime if she "attempts to kill or knowingly causes or attempts to cause serious physical injury to another person." § 565.050.1. The charge against Defendant was submitted to the jury on a theory of accomplice liability. See § 562.041.1. The underlying premise for this statutory form of criminal liability is that all persons who act in concert to commit a crime are equally guilty. See State v. Meuir , 138 S.W.3d 137, 143 (Mo. App. 2004). "Missouri eliminated the distinction between principals and accessories in 1979, and it is now the law that all persons who act in concert are equally guilty." State v. Barnum , 14 S.W.3d 587, 591 (Mo. banc 2000) ; see State v. Turrentine , 524 S.W.3d 55, 60 (Mo. App. 2016). Pursuant to an accomplice liability theory, a person is "criminally responsible" for the conduct of another when "[e]ither before or during the commission of an offense with the purpose of promoting the commission of an offense, he aids or agrees to aid or attempts to aid such other person in planning, committing or attempting to commit the offense." § 562.041.1(2). Thus, "[a]n accomplice is one who, before or during the commission of a crime, intentionally and knowingly aids or encourages the commission of a crime[.]" Meuir , 138 S.W.3d at 143.

label: qa_CONTRACTS_mbe_783
subject: CONTRACTS
question: On June 1, Topline Wholesale, Inc., received a purchase-order form from Wonder-Good, Inc., a retailer and new customer, in which the latter ordered 1,000 anti-recoil widgets for delivery no later than August 30 at a delivered total price of $10,000, as quoted in Topline's current catalog. Both parties are merchants with respect to widgets of all types. On June 2, Topline mailed to Wonder-Good its own form, across the top of which Topline's president had written, "We are pleased to accept your order." This form contained the same terms as Wonder-Good's form except for an additional printed clause in Topline's form that provided for a maximum liability of $100 for any breach of contract by Topline. As of June 5, when Wonder-Good received Topline's acceptance form, which of the following is an accurate statement concerning the legal relationship between Topline and Wonder-Good?
final_answer:
To determine the legal relationship between Topline and Wonder-Good as of June 5, we need to analyze the transaction under the Uniform Commercial Code (U.C.C.), specifically section 2-207, which deals with the addition of terms in a contract between merchants.

1. **Offer and Acceptance**: Wonder-Good sent a purchase order to Topline, which constitutes an offer. Topline then accepted this offer by mailing its own form with the notation "We are pleased to accept your order." This indicates that Topline accepted Wonder-Good's offer.

2. **Additional Terms**: Topline's acceptance form included an additional clause not present in Wonder-Good's offer—a maximum liability of $100 for any breach of contract by Topline. Under U.C.C. section 2-207, in a contract between merchants, additional terms become part of the contract unless they materially alter the contract, the offeror expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the offer, or the offeror objects to the additional terms within a reasonable time.

3. **Material Alteration**: The key issue here is whether the liability-limitation clause materially alters the contract. A material alteration is one that would result in surprise or hardship to one of the parties if it were included without express notice. Limiting liability to $100 for any breach could significantly impact the contractual relationship, potentially causing surprise or hardship to Wonder-Good, especially considering the total contract price is $10,000.

4. **Analysis of Choices**:
    - **(A)** This choice suggests there is no contract because the liability-limitation clause is a material alteration. Given that both parties are merchants and the clause could cause surprise or hardship, this is a plausible interpretation under U.C.C. section 2-207.
    - **(B)** This choice implies that without express consent to the additional term, there can be no contract. However, U.C.C. section 2-207 allows for the inclusion of additional terms under certain conditions, even without express consent.
    - **(C)** This choice is incorrect because while the U.C.C. does allow for liquidation of damages, the inclusion of such a clause as part of the contract depends on whether it materially alters the contract, not merely because it is authorized by the U.C.C.
    - **(D)** This choice suggests that there is a contract, but it does not include the liability-limitation clause. This could be correct if the clause is considered a material alteration, as it would not be included in the contract under U.C.C. section 2-207.

Given the analysis, the most accurate statement concerning the legal relationship between Topline and Wonder-Good as of June 5 is that there is an enforceable contract, but the terms do not include the liability-limitation clause because it materially alters the contract. This aligns with the principles outlined in U.C.C. section 2-207 regarding the inclusion of additional terms in contracts between merchants.

Answer: (D)

choices.A: There is no contract, because the liability-limitation clause in Topline's form is a material alteration of WonderGood's offer.
choices.B: There is no contract, because WonderGood did not consent to the liabilitylimitation clause in Topline's form.
choices.C: There is an enforceable contract whose terms include the liability-limitation clause in Topline's form, because liquidation of damages is expressly authorized by the Uniform Commercial Code.
choices.D: There is an enforceable contract whose terms do not include the liabilitylimitation clause in Topline's form.

Because a material alteration is an affirmative defense, the burden is on the surety to demonstrate that a material alteration occurred. See Sonne v. Federal Deposit Ins. Corp., 881 S.W.2d 789, 793 (Tex.App.—Houston [14th Dist.] 1994, writ denied) (citing Federal Deposit Ins. Corp. v. Attayi, 745 S.W.2d 939, 944 (Tex.App.—Houston [1st Dist.] 1988, no writ).

snap_answer:
Under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), which governs contracts for the sale of goods between merchants, a written acceptance that states additional or different terms will operate as an acceptance (thereby creating a contract) unless the offeror expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the offer. However, if the additional or different terms in the acceptance are material, the offeror is free to reject them, and the contract will consist of the terms of the offer.

In this scenario, Wonder-Good, the buyer, submitted a purchase order for 1,000 anti-recoil widgets with specific terms, including a total price and delivery date. Topline, the seller, responded with its own form that accepted the order but included an additional clause limiting Topline's liability to $100 for any breach of contract. This clause can be considered a material alteration because it significantly affects the risk allocation between the parties.

Given that both parties are merchants, the UCC's provisions regarding the battle of the forms apply. According to UCC §2-207, when a merchant's acceptance includes additional or different terms, such terms become part of the contract unless:

1. The offer expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the offer.
2. The additional or different terms materially alter the contract.
3. The offeror objects to the additional or different terms within a reasonable time.

In this case, since the liability-limitation clause can be seen as a material alteration (it significantly affects the contractual risk between the parties), and there's no indication that Wonder-Good expressly agreed to this term or that it was expressly authorized by the UCC as a standard term in such contracts, the contract would consist of the terms agreed upon by both parties initially, excluding the liability-limitation clause.

Therefore, there is an enforceable contract, but its terms do not include the liability-limitation clause in Topline's form because that clause materially alters the contract, and there's no indication that Wonder-Good accepted it.

Answer: (D)
hyde_passage: Under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), specifically §2-207, a written acceptance that includes additional or different terms will operate as an acceptance, but the additional terms will not become part of the contract if they materially alter the contract. The UCC provides that between merchants, additional terms become part of the contract unless they materially alter it, or the offeror objects to them within a reasonable time. In cases where the additional terms are considered material alterations, such as a clause limiting liability for breach of contract, these terms will not be included in the contract if the offeror does not expressly agree to them.

evidence_store.text: Under the U.C.C., in a deal between merchants, additional terms in the acceptance or in the written confirmation will be included as part of the deal unless the additional terms materially alter the original deal, the offer expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the offer, or the offeror objects to the terms within a reasonable time [U.C.C. 2-207(2)]

At common law, any different or additional terms in an acceptance of an offer made the response a rejection and counteroffer. In contracts for the sale of goods, however, U.C.C. section 2-207 substantially alters the common law rule. The proposal of additional terms by the offeree in a definite and timely acceptance does not constitute a rejection and counteroffer. Rather, the acceptance is effective unless it is expressly made conditional on assent to the additional terms. If both parties to the sale of goods contract are merchants, the additional terms will become part of the contract unless (i) they materially alter the contract, (ii) the offer expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the offer, or (iii) the offeror has already objected to the particular terms, or he objects within a reasonable time after notice of them is received.

In a contract between merchants under U.C.C. section 2-201, a writing in confirmation of the contract (even if it varies from the original offer) that is sufficient to bind the sender will also satisfy the Statute of Frauds against the recipient unless a written objection is made within 10 days. Under Article 2, an acceptance containing different or additional terms is still effective as an acceptance unless made expressly conditional on assent to those terms.

Under U.C.C. section 2-207, a written confirmation is effective as an acceptance even though it states additional terms, unless the acceptance is expressly made conditional on assent to the additional terms.

Shoring's primary argument is that forum-selection clauses are material, and therefore require express assent to become part of a contract under UCC § 2-207. Dkt. 5 at 6; Tex. Bus. & Comm. Code Ann. § 2.207(b)(2). However, § 2-207 applies only to material terms that are proposed after a contract has formed. See Tex. Bus. & Comm. Code Ann. § 2.207(a). Because the GTCs were part of J.D. Fields's initial offer, § 2-207 does not apply.

label: qa_TORTS_mbe_752
subject: TORTS
question: Peavey was walking peacefully along a public street when he encountered Dorwin, whom he had never seen before. Without provocation or warning, Dorwin picked up a rock and struck Peavey with it. It was later established that Dorwin was mentally ill and suffered recurrent hallucinations. If Peavey asserts a claim against Dorwin based on battery, which of the following, if supported by evidence, will be Dorwin's best defense?
final_answer:
To determine Dorwin's best defense in a claim of battery by Peavey, we need to consider the elements of battery and how they apply to the given scenario. Battery requires an act by the defendant that brings about a harmful or offensive contact to the plaintiff's person, intent on the defendant's part to bring about harmful or offensive contact, and causation. The intent element is satisfied if the defendant knew with substantial certainty that the harmful or offensive contact would result from his actions.

Let's analyze the options given:

(A) Dorwin did not understand that his act was wrongful.
- This option addresses Dorwin's understanding of the wrongfulness of his act, which relates to his mental state. However, under the majority view, as stated in Source 2, mental incompetency does not preclude a showing that a defendant "intended" to inflict a harmful or offensive touching. Thus, not understanding the act was wrongful due to mental illness may not be a strong defense against the intent required for battery.

(B) Dorwin did not desire to cause harm to Peavey.
- Desire to cause harm is not the standard for intent in battery. The intent required is to bring about harmful or offensive contact, which can be satisfied if the defendant knew with substantial certainty that such contact would result, regardless of his desire for harm.

(C) Dorwin did not know that he was striking a person.
- This option directly addresses the intent element. If Dorwin did not know he was striking a person, he could not have intended or known with substantial certainty that a harmful or offensive contact with a person would result, as stated in Source 3. This lack of knowledge would undermine the intent element necessary for a battery claim.

(D) Dorwin thought Peavey was about to attack him.
- This option suggests a possible self-defense claim, which could justify Dorwin's actions. However, the question specifically asks for the best defense against a battery claim, not a justification for the action. Self-defense might be relevant but does not directly address the intent element required for battery.

Given the analysis, the best defense for Dorwin against a battery claim, if supported by evidence, would be that he did not know he was striking a person. This directly impacts the intent element required for battery, as one cannot intend to bring about harmful or offensive contact if they are unaware that their actions will result in such contact with a person.

Answer: (C)

choices.A: Dorwin did not understand that his act was wrongful.
choices.B: Dorwin did not desire to cause harm to Peavey.
choices.C: Dorwin did not know that he was striking a person.
choices.D: Dorwin thought Peavey was about to attack him.

Ransweiler, 89 Cal. Rptr. 3d 801, 811 (Ct. App. 2009) (citing Piedra v. Dugan, 21 Cal. Rptr. 3d 36, 48 (Ct. App. 2004) ). “A harmful contact, intentionally done is the essence of a battery.” Ashc

To determine the best defense for Dorwin in a claim of battery by Peavey, we need to understand the elements of battery and how mental illness or lack of understanding might affect liability.

Battery is a tort that consists of three main elements:
1. An act by the defendant that brings about harmful or offensive contact to the plaintiff.
2. The act must be done intentionally. This means the defendant either desired to cause the contact or knew with substantial certainty that the contact would result from his actions.
3. The contact must be harmful or offensive.

Given that Dorwin is mentally ill and suffers from recurrent hallucinations, his best defense would hinge on whether his mental state affected his ability to form the intent required for battery.

Let's analyze the options:
(A) "Dorwin did not understand that his act was wrongful." - This statement addresses Dorwin's understanding of the wrongfulness of his act but does not directly address the intent element of battery.
(B) "Dorwin did not desire to cause harm to Peavey." - While this might seem relevant, the intent for battery can also be satisfied if Dorwin knew with substantial certainty that his action would cause contact, even if he didn't desire harm.
(C) "Dorwin did not know that he was striking a person." - This option directly addresses the intent element. If Dorwin, due to his hallucinations, genuinely believed he was not striking a person, he lacked the necessary intent for battery. This would be a strong defense because it challenges the second element of battery.
(D) "Dorwin thought Peavey was about to attack him." - This could potentially lead to a self-defense claim, but it implies that Dorwin had some awareness of Peavey's presence and an intention to act against him, which might not be as strong a defense as challenging the intent element directly due to a lack of understanding of his actions.

Considering these options, the best defense for Dorwin, given his mental illness and the elements of battery, would be to argue that he did not have the requisite intent due to his lack of understanding of his actions.

Answer: (C)

evidence_store.text: A prima facie case for battery requires plaintiff to prove (i) an act by defendant that brings about a harmful or offensive contact to the plaintiff's person, (ii) intent on defendant's part to bring about harmful or offensive contact, and (iii) causation. The intent element is satisfied as long as the defendant knew with substantial certainty that the harmful or offensive contact would result. If the defendant had only intended to cause apprehension of contact, which is the intent for assault, this intent would suffice for liability for battery under the doctrine of transferred intent.

Battery requires an intent on the part of the defendant to bring about harmful or offensive (i.e., unconsented-to) contact to the plaintiff's person. Under the majority view, minors and incompetents will be liable for their intentional torts. Mental incompetency does not preclude a showing that a defendant "intended" to inflict a harmful or offensive touching.

To establish a prima facie case for an intentional tort such as battery, the plaintiff must prove (i) an act by the defendant, (ii) intent, and (iii) causation. The intent of the actor that is relevant for purposes of intentional torts is the intent to bring about the consequences that are the basis of the tort. An actor "intends" these consequences when his goal is to bring them about or when he knows with substantial certainty that they will result from his actions. The intent required for battery is an intent to bring about harmful or offensive contact to the plaintiff's person. [Restatement (Second) of Torts 13(a)] If the defendant did not know that he was striking a person, he could not have intended or known that a harmful or offensive contact with the person would result.

The mental state necessary for criminal battery is general intent, which is satisfied by intentional, reckless, or criminally negligent conduct. A serious injury is not required for battery. Criminal negligence requires a greater deviation from ordinary behavior than simply a "failure to exercise due care," which is the tort standard.

¶55 Under existing case law, the medical malpractice statute does not supersede the common law cause of action for medical battery. Bundrick v. Stewart , 128 Wash. App. 11, 16-17, 114 P.3d 1204 (2005) ; see also Young , 155 Wash. App. at 821-23, 230 P.3d 222. A battery is an intentional harmful or offensive bodily contact with another person. Kumar v. Gate Gourmet, Inc. , 180 Wash.2d 481, 504, 325 P.3d 193 (2014). A person is liable for battery if he or she intends to cause a harmful or offensive contact and such a contact directly or indirectly results. Id. The intent element is satisfied if a defendant knows to a substantial certainty that his or her actions will result in the contact. In other words, the requisite intent for battery is to cause an offensive contact, not an injury. Sutton , 180 Wash. App. at 866, 324 P.3d 763.

label: qa_CONTRACTS_mbe_996
subject: CONTRACTS
question: On July 15, in a writing signed by both parties, Fixtures, Inc., agreed to deliver to Druggist on August 15 five storage cabinets from inventory for a total price of $5,000 to be paid on delivery. On August 1, the two parties orally agreed to postpone the delivery date to August 20. On August 20, Fixtures tendered the cabinets to Druggist, who refused to accept or pay for them on the ground that they were not tendered on August 15, even though they otherwise met the contract specifications. Assuming that all appropriate defenses are seasonably raised, will Fixtures succeed in an action against Druggist for breach of contract?
final_answer:
To determine whether Fixtures will succeed in an action against Druggist for breach of contract, we need to analyze the situation step by step.

1. **Initial Contract**: The initial contract between Fixtures, Inc., and Druggist, signed on July 15, specified that Fixtures would deliver five storage cabinets to Druggist on August 15 for a total price of $5,000. This contract is enforceable as it meets the basic requirements of a contract (offer, acceptance, consideration, etc.).

2. **Modification of the Contract**: On August 1, the parties orally agreed to modify the delivery date from August 15 to August 20. The key issue here is whether this oral modification is enforceable.

3. **Consideration for Modification**: At common law, a modification of a contract requires consideration to be enforceable because the parties are under a preexisting duty to perform. However, under the UCC, a contract modification does not require additional consideration to be binding (13 P.S. § 2209(a)). The question does not specify whether the UCC applies, but given the nature of the transaction (involving the sale of goods), it's plausible that the UCC could apply, potentially making the modification enforceable without additional consideration.

4. **Enforceability of the Modification**: Even if we consider the common law requirement for consideration, the modification could still be enforceable under the doctrine of promissory estoppel if Fixtures relied on Druggist's promise to accept the cabinets on the new delivery date and if enforcing the promise is necessary to prevent injustice.

5. **Parol Evidence Rule**: The parol evidence rule does not apply to subsequent modifications of a contract. Therefore, evidence of the August 1 oral agreement can be introduced to prove the modification of the delivery date.

6. **Waiver**: Druggist's refusal to accept or pay for the cabinets on August 20, based on the ground that they were not tendered on August 15, suggests an attempt to revert to the original terms. However, having agreed to the modification, Druggist is bound by the new delivery date unless there's a valid reason to revert or terminate the contract, which is not indicated in the scenario.

Given these considerations, Fixtures should succeed in an action against Druggist for breach of contract because the oral modification of the delivery date is enforceable, either under the UCC or through promissory estoppel, and Druggist's refusal to accept the cabinets on the modified delivery date constitutes a breach.

Answer: (B)

Further, according to Florida law, “[c]onduct may constitute waiver of a contract term, but such an implied waiver must be demonstrated by clear evidence.” BMC Indus., Inc. v. snap_answer:
To determine whether Fixtures will succeed in an action against Druggist for breach of contract, let's analyze the situation step by step:

1. **Original Contract**: The original contract between Fixtures, Inc., and Druggist, signed on July 15, specified that Fixtures would deliver five storage cabinets to Druggist on August 15 for a total price of $5,000, to be paid on delivery. This contract is in writing and outlines the terms of the agreement, including the delivery date.

2. **Modification of the Contract**: On August 1, the two parties orally agreed to postpone the delivery date to August 20. This is a modification of the original contract. The key issue here is whether this oral modification is enforceable.

3. **Consideration for Modification**: For a contract modification to be enforceable, it typically requires consideration, which means something of value must be given in exchange for the modification. However, in the context of contract modification, the requirement of consideration can sometimes be waived or implied, especially when the modification is made in good faith and before any breach has occurred.

4. **Waiver and Estoppel**: The August 1 agreement to postpone the delivery can be seen as a waiver of the original August 15 delivery term. By agreeing to the new delivery date, Druggist effectively waived the right to insist on the original delivery date. This waiver can be considered a form of consideration, as both parties are altering their positions in reliance on the new agreement.

5. **Parol Evidence Rule**: The parol evidence rule does not apply here because the oral agreement of August 1 is being used to modify, not contradict, the terms of the written contract. The rule generally prohibits the use of extrinsic evidence to vary or contradict the terms of a written contract, but modifications agreed upon by the parties after the contract's formation are exceptions to this rule.

6. **Breach of Contract**: Druggist's refusal to accept or pay for the cabinets on August 20, after having agreed to the postponed delivery date, constitutes a breach of contract. By refusing to perform according to the modified terms, Druggist is failing to uphold their end of the agreement.

Given these considerations, Fixtures should succeed in an action against Druggist for breach of contract because the August 1 agreement operated as a waiver of the August 15 delivery term. Druggist's agreement to postpone the delivery date and subsequent refusal to accept the cabinets as per the new agreement constitutes a breach of the modified contract terms.

Answer: (B)

According to the principles of contract law, a modification to a contract requires consideration to be enforceable, but an exception exists when the modification is made in good faith and before any breach has occurred, in which case the requirement of consideration may be waived or implied. The waiver doctrine also applies, where a party's voluntary relinquishment of a known right, such as an agreed-upon delivery date, can serve as consideration for a contract modification. In this context, the parol evidence rule does not bar the introduction of evidence regarding the oral modification agreement, as it is being used to modify, rather than contradict, the terms of the written contract. 

Answer: (B)

evidence_store.text: At common law, a modification of a contract required consideration because the parties were under a preexisting legal duty to perform. The parol evidence rule does not apply to subsequent modifications.

Under the preexisting legal duty rule, the promise to perform or the performance of an existing legal duty will not be sufficient consideration. If the parties agree to modify their contract, consideration is usually found to exist where the obligations of both parties are varied. However, a modification solely for the benefit of one of the parties is unenforceable in contracts not governed by the U.C.C. Contracts for services do not come within the Statute of Frauds unless by their terms they cannot be performed within one year of their making, and performance of this contract could be completed in less than a year. Even where unsupported by consideration, a promise is enforceable under the promissory estoppel doctrine to the extent necessary to prevent injustice if: (i) the promisor should reasonably expect to induce action or forbearance of a definite and substantial character; and (ii) such action or forbearance is in fact induced.

“A contract can be modified when there is clear and convincing evidence of the parties' mutual intent to modify the contract through their course of dealing.” Third Fed. S. & L. Assn. of Cleveland v. Formanik, 8th Dist. Cuyahoga Nos. 100562 & 100810, 2014-Ohio-3234, 2014 WL 3700514, ¶ 13, citing Westgate Ford Truck Sales, Inc. v. Ford Motor Co., 2012-Ohio-1942, 971 N.E.2d 967, ¶¶ 24–25 (8th Dist.); see also RotoSolutions, Inc. v. Crane Plastics Siding, L.L.C., 10th Dist. Franklin Nos. 13AP-1 & 13AP–52, 2013-Ohio-4343, 2013 WL 5451702, ¶ 19 (“ ‘[A]n oral modification of a written contract can be enforceable notwithstanding a provision in the contract requiring modifications to be in writing where * * * the parties have engaged in a course of conduct in conformance with the oral modification and where the party seeking to enforce the oral modification would suffer injury if the modification were deemed invalid.’ ”), quoting Exact Software N. Am., Inc. v. Infocon Sys., Inc., N.D.Ohio No. 3:03CV7183, 2004 WL 952876 (Apr. 16, 2004); Miller Lakes Community Servs. Assn., Inc. v. Schmitt, 9th Dist. Wayne, 2016-Ohio-339, 2016 WL 515641, ¶¶ 18, 30 (“ ‘Parties may implicitly modify an agreement by their actions. A continued, different, course of performance between parties manifests a modification of the original agreement.’ ”), quoting St. Marys v. Auglaize Cty. Bd. of Commrs., 115 Ohio St.3d 387, 2007-Ohio-5026, 875 N.E.2d 561, ¶ 39; Kwikcolor Sand v. Fairmount Minerals Ltd., 8th Dist. Cuyahoga No. 96717, 2011-Ohio-6646, 2011 WL 6775580, ¶¶ 20–22 (where it was undisputed that the parties orally agreed to modify contract's pricing schedule and operated under the modified pricing schedule for more than a year, plaintiff waived the contract's no-oral modification provision and the parties were contractually bound to the modified pricing structure).

When determining the scope of a modification to a contract, the following principles control: (1) "individual terms of a contract are not to be considered in isolation, but as a whole and in relation to one another"; (2) "the proper resolution of any inconsistency ... is best determined by the manner in which the parties actually perform under it"; and (3) "an amendment to an agreement is designed to serve some useful function, and its existence is strong evidence, therefore, that the contract was changed from what the parties believed and intended was provided before." S. Fla. Beverage Corp. v. Figueredo , 409 So.2d 490, 495-96 (Fla. 3d DCA 1981). "When it is clear from the language or overall scheme or plan of a contract that the parties intended for various provisions to operate independently of each other, the court must enforce the contract as written." Moore v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co. , 916 So.2d 871, 875 (Fla. 2d DCA 2005).

The first issue to address is which contract terms control: the Initial or the Updated Terms and Conditions. To sufficiently plead the existence of a contract, the plaintiff must allege facts showing that (1) "both parties manifested an intention to be bound by the agreement," (2) "the terms of the agreement are sufficiently definite," and (3) there was consideration. Blair v. Scott Specialty Gases , 283 F.3d 595, 603 (3d Cir. 2002). Consideration, of course, is a bargained-for exchange between the parties, Cornell Narberth, LLC v. Borough of Narberth , 167 A.3d 228, 237 (Pa. Commw. Ct. 2017), and it is often formed by mutual promises, see Bethlehem Area Sch. Dist. v. Zhou , 2012 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 37313, at *6 (E.D. Pa. Mar. 19, 2012) ("It is a general principle of contract law that mutual promises are valid consideration."). Under Pennsylvania common law, once a contract is formed, additional consideration is required to modify the contract. Greishaw v. Base Mfg. , 2008 WL 509077, at *4, 2008 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 13066, at *12 (W.D. Pa. Feb. 21, 2008). On the other hand, if the UCC, rather than common law, applies to a contract, then an agreement modifying the contract does not need consideration in order to be binding. 13 P.S. § 2209(a). Here, Plaintiffs allege that the Updated Terms and Conditions are unenforceable due to lack of consideration. (ECF No. 31, at ¶ 13 ). Defendant Sperian Energy argues that, under the UCC, consideration is not necessary for contract modifications. (ECF No. 49, at 6 ). However, both sides seem to misunderstand the nature of their contractual relationship.

question: If Farber asserts a claim against Acme Company for the loss of his lettuce crop will Farber prevail?
final_answer:
To determine if Farber will prevail in his claim against Acme Company for the loss of his lettuce crop, we need to analyze the situation based on the principles of liability, particularly focusing on strict liability, negligence, and the conditions under which a defendant can be held liable for damages.

1. **Strict Liability**: This principle holds a defendant liable for injuries or damages caused by their actions or products, regardless of fault or intent, if the activity or product is deemed abnormally dangerous or defective. For strict liability to apply, the activity must be inherently dangerous, even if all necessary precautions are taken.

2. **Negligence**: To prove negligence, the plaintiff must show that the defendant owed a duty of care, breached that duty, and the breach caused the plaintiff's injuries. The defendant's knowledge of a defect or dangerous condition and failure to act can be crucial in establishing negligence.

3. **Products Liability**: If the claim involves a product (e.g., a storage facility), the plaintiff must prove that the product was defective, the defect caused the injury, and the defendant was involved in the product's manufacture, distribution, or sale.

Given the options provided:

- **(A) No, if Acme did not design the storage facility.** This option does not directly address the principles of liability that would apply in this scenario. The design of the facility could be relevant, but it's not the sole determining factor in liability.

- **(B) No, because Acme was an independent contractor.** Being an independent contractor might affect the contractual relationships and possibly the insurance or indemnification obligations, but it does not inherently absolve Acme of liability for damages caused by their actions or omissions, especially under strict liability or negligence theories.

- **(C) Yes, because the operation of the storage facility was an abnormally dangerous activity.** This option suggests that the activity itself (operating the storage facility) is inherently dangerous, which could impose strict liability on Acme if the operation of the facility led to the loss of Farber's lettuce crop, regardless of whether Acme was negligent.

- **(D) Yes, if the explosion resulted from a defect of which Acme was aware.** This option implies negligence on Acme's part. If Acme knew about a defect and failed to address it, leading to the explosion and subsequent loss, they could be held liable for Farber's damages.

Based on the information provided and the principles of liability, the most direct path to establishing liability would involve proving that Acme's actions or the condition of the storage facility (if under their control) directly caused the damage. Without specific details on the nature of the loss (e.g., whether it was due to an explosion, a defect in the facility, or another cause), option **(D)** seems to offer a plausible scenario under which Farber could prevail, as it directly addresses a situation where Acme's knowledge and failure to act on a defect could lead to liability.

Answer: (D)

evidence_store.text: Regarding DOTD's liability, the pertinent legal principles are somewhat different insofar as tort claims may be pursued against the public entity in strict liability pursuant to La. C.C. art. 2317 and La. R.S. 9:2800, as well as in negligence pursuant to La. C.C. art. 2315. Netecke, 747 So.2d at 494. When addressing an action under either theory, the legal analysis is the same. The plaintiff bears the burden of showing that: (1) DOTD had custody of the thing that caused the plaintiff's injuries or damages; (2) the thing was defective because it had a condition that created an unreasonable risk of harm; (3) DOTD had actual or constructive knowledge of the defect and did not take corrective measures within a reasonable time; and (4) the defect in the thing was a cause-in-fact of the plaintiff's injuries. Netecke, 747 So.2d at 494.

In a products liability action based on defect, a plaintiff must prove by a preponderance of the evidence that (1) the defendant placed the product on the market for use and knew, or in the exercise of reasonable care should have known, that the product would be used without inspection for defects; (2) the product was in a defective condition when it was placed on the market and left the defendant's possession; (3) the defect is the proximate or a proximately contributing cause of the plaintiff's injury sustained while the product was being used in a way and for the general purpose for which it was designed and intended; (4) the defect, if existent, rendered the product unreasonably dangerous and unsafe for its intended use; and (5) the plaintiff's damages were a direct and proximate result of the alleged defect.

"(a) knows or by the exercise of reasonable care would discover the condition, and should realize that it involves an unreasonable risk of harm to such invitees, and (b) should expect that they will not discover or realize the danger, or will fail to protect themselves against it, and (c) fails to exercise reasonable care to protect them against the danger." Restatement (Second) of Torts § 343 (1965).

Under [A]rticle 2317.1, a defect is a condition creating an "unreasonable risk of harm," which requires the trier of fact to decide whether the social value and utility of a hazard outweighs, and thus justifies, its potential harm to others. The fact that a pedestrian fell on a walkway does not automatically elevate the condition of that walkway to that of an unreasonably dangerous defect. The defect must be of such a nature to constitute a dangerous condition, which would reasonably be expected to cause injury to a prudent person using ordinary care under the circumstances.

To establish a prima facie case in products liability based on strict liability in tort, the following elements must be proved: (i) strict duty owed by a commercial supplier; (ii) production or sale of a defective product; (iii) actual and proximate cause; and (iv) damages. To establish the second element, the plaintiff need prove only that the product in fact is so defective as to be "unreasonably dangerous." In failure to warn cases, the plaintiff is entitled to a presumption that an adequate warning would have been read and heeded, although the jury could very well reject that presumption. Nevertheless, whether inadequate warnings made the product so defective as to be unreasonably dangerous is a question of fact for the jury.

question: Alice conveyed Twinoaks Farm "to Barbara, her heirs and assigns, so long as the premises are used for residential and farm purposes, then to Charles and his heirs and assigns." The jurisdiction in which Twinoaks Farm is located has adopted the common-law Rule Against Perpetuities unmodified by statute. As a consequence of the conveyance, Alice's interest in Twinoaks Farm is
final_answer:
To determine Alice's interest in Twinoaks Farm after the conveyance, let's analyze the given information step by step.

1. **Type of Estate Conveyed**: The conveyance is "to Barbara, her heirs and assigns, so long as the premises are used for residential and farm purposes." This language indicates a fee simple determinable because it includes durational language ("so long as"), which is a requirement for creating a fee simple determinable. The condition ("used for residential and farm purposes") is the event that, if it ceases to occur, will trigger the termination of Barbara's estate.

2. **Future Interest in Alice**: After conveying a fee simple determinable, the grantor (Alice, in this case) retains a future interest known as a possibility of reverter. This interest means that if the condition ("used for residential and farm purposes") is not met, the property will automatically revert to Alice.

3. **Comparison with Other Options**:
   - **(A) Nothing**: This is incorrect because Alice does retain an interest after the conveyance.
   - **(B) A possibility of reverter**: This matches our analysis. Alice has a possibility of reverter because she conveyed a fee simple determinable.
   - **(C) A right of entry for condition broken**: This is a different type of future interest. A right of entry (or a fee simple subject to a condition subsequent) requires the grantor to take affirmative action to regain the property if the condition is broken, whereas a possibility of reverter involves automatic reversion.
   - **(D) A reversion in fee simple absolute**: A reversion occurs when a grantor transfers a lesser estate than they own, and the property automatically reverts to the grantor upon the termination of the granted estate. However, in this case, Alice conveyed a fee simple determinable, which suggests she retained a possibility of reverter, not a reversion.

Given the analysis, Alice's interest in Twinoaks Farm after the conveyance is best described as a possibility of reverter because she conveyed a fee simple determinable and thus retains the interest that automatically reverts to her if the specified condition is not met.

Answer: (B)

choices.A: nothing
choices.B: a possibility of reverter
choices.C: a right of entry for condition broken
choices.D: a reversion in fee simple absolute

evidence_store.text: possibility of a reverter: The possibility of reverter is a future interest held by a grantor or transferor of property as a fee simple determinable (See possessory estate). Distinct from a fee simple absolute, a fee simple determinable is an estate that has a provision that automatically reverts the property to the grantor if an event happens. The event (or nonevent) that triggers the automatic transfer of property back to the grantor is called the condition subsequent. Thus, the possibility of reverter is the interest held by the grantor that may or may not be triggered and leads to automatic restoration of ownership in the grantor/transferror. This is also distinct from the right of entry, which is a future interest held by the grantor that allows, but does not require, the grantor to restore ownership (See fee simple subject to condition subsequent). For example, Blackacre grants “the property to A and her heirs so long as the property is used for educational purposes.” After granting this fee simple determinable, Blackacre maintains a possibility of reverter in the property. If A uses the property for a purpose other than education, the property reverts to grantor Blackacre.

A possibility of reverter is “a future interest retained by a grantor after conveying a fee simple determinable, so that the grantee's estate terminates automatically and reverts to the grantor if the terminating event ever occurs.” BLACK'S LAW DICTIONARY 1284 (9th ed.2009).
A reversionary interest is an interest remaining in the grantor. A possibility of reverter is the interest left in the grantor after a conveyance of a fee simple determinable.

The language in the deed merely expresses the grantor's motive for conveying the property. A fee simple determinable is an estate that automatically terminates on the happening of a stated event. To create a fee simple determinable, durational language (e.g., "for so long as," "until") must be used. If the interest is not a fee simple determinable, the grantor cannot have a possibility of reverter.

The language of the grant creates a fee simple subject to a condition subsequent in one person and an executory interest in the other person. If a condition subsequent attempts to restrict the transferability of a fee simple, the forfeiture restriction is an invalid restraint on alienation and therefore is void.

label: qa_nan_mbe_346

question: Acting on an anonymous telephone call, police went to Desmond's apartment, knocked on the door, and demanded to search it for narcotics. When Desmond refused, the police forced the door open and placed him under arrest. As they were removing him from the apartment, Desmond offered to give the officers "valuable information" in exchange for his release. Before he could say anything else, Desmond was given Miranda warnings by the police. Thereafter he told the police that he had stored some heroin in his friend's apartment and that he and his friend had been going to sell it. The heroin was recovered, and Desmond was prosecuted for conspiracy to sell narcotics and for possession of narcotics. At his trial, Desmond moved to suppress his statements. Which of the following is Desmond's best argument in support of the motion to suppress?
final_answer:
To determine Desmond's best argument in support of the motion to suppress, let's analyze each option in light of the provided information and relevant legal principles.

(A) This option suggests that Desmond's right to know the identity of his accuser is violated because the tip was anonymous. However, the right to confront one's accusers primarily applies at trial, not at the pre-trial stage of gathering evidence. Thus, this argument may not directly support a motion to suppress statements made after the arrest.

(B) Miranda warnings are required to protect the Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination and must be given before a custodial interrogation. The scenario indicates that Desmond was given Miranda warnings after he was placed under arrest and before he made any incriminating statements. The timing of the warnings in relation to the entry into the apartment is not the critical factor; what matters is that they were given before the interrogation. Therefore, this argument may not be the strongest.

(C) This option argues that Desmond's statements were involuntary due to the coercive circumstances of the forced entry and arrest. The Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment prohibits the admission of involuntary statements. If Desmond can show that the forced entry and his subsequent arrest created a coercive environment that undermined his ability to make a voluntary statement, this could be a strong argument for suppressing the statements. The key here is whether the totality of the circumstances, including the forced entry and the offer of "valuable information" in exchange for release, rendered Desmond's statements involuntary.

(D) This option suggests that even if the Miranda warnings were sufficient to protect Desmond's right against self-incrimination, they could not purge the taint of an unlawful arrest. However, the legality of the arrest is not explicitly challenged in the scenario provided, and the focus is on the voluntariness of the statements made after the arrest.

Considering the principles outlined in the retrieved passages, particularly the emphasis on the totality of the circumstances in determining voluntariness (Source 1, Source 2, Source 3, Source 4, Source 5), Desmond's best argument would be that the circumstances under which he was arrested and the statements were made were so coercive that his will was overborne, and thus, his statements were involuntary.

Answer: (C)

choices.A: Desmond is entitled to know the identity of his accuser, and the state cannot supply this information.
choices.B: The police should have given Desmond Miranda warnings prior to entry into the apartment, and the warnings were ineffectual once Desmond offered to give the police information.
choices.C: Desmond was intimidated by the forced entry into the apartment, and since the statements were involuntary and coerced, their use against him would violate due process of law.
choices.D: The statements were fruits of an unlawful arrest, and though the Miranda warnings may have been sufficient to protect his right against self-incrimination, they were not sufficient to purge the taint of the illegal arrest.

snap_answer:
To determine Desmond's best argument in support of the motion to suppress, let's analyze each option step by step:

(A) This option suggests that Desmond's right to know his accuser is violated because the tip was anonymous. However, the right to know one's accuser is primarily relevant in the context of confrontation at trial, not in the context of suppressing statements made during a police encounter. This does not directly address the issue of the statements made after the Miranda warnings.

(B) Miranda warnings are required to be given before a custodial interrogation. The scenario indicates that Desmond was placed under arrest and thus was in custody. However, the critical issue here is not the timing of the Miranda warnings in relation to the entry into the apartment but whether the warnings were effective in protecting Desmond's rights after he was already in custody and had expressed a willingness to provide information. The warnings given after Desmond offered to provide information could be seen as sufficient to protect his right against self-incrimination, assuming the interrogation that followed was not coercive.

(C) This option focuses on the voluntariness of Desmond's statements. If Desmond can show that his statements were made under circumstances that made them involuntary (e.g., due to the intimidating nature of the forced entry), he might have a strong argument for suppression. The key here is whether the forced entry and subsequent arrest created an environment where Desmond's statements were not truly voluntary. This argument directly addresses the potential psychological impact of the police actions on Desmond's decision to make statements.

(D) This option suggests that even if the Miranda warnings were sufficient to protect Desmond's right against self-incrimination, the initial arrest was unlawful, and thus any statements made thereafter (even with Miranda warnings) are "fruits of the poisonous tree" and should be suppressed. However, for this argument to succeed, Desmond would need to establish that the arrest was indeed unlawful, which is not clearly the case from the information provided. The legality of the arrest depends on factors not fully detailed in the scenario, such as whether the police had reasonable suspicion or probable cause for the arrest.

Considering these options, Desmond's best argument would likely focus on the voluntariness of his statements, given the circumstances of the forced entry and arrest. This directly challenges the admissibility of the statements based on the potential coercive environment created by the police actions.

Answer: (C)
hyde_passage:
According to the due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment, a statement made by a defendant is admissible as evidence against him only if it was made voluntarily, and the voluntariness of a statement is determined by considering the totality of the circumstances surrounding its making. If a statement is found to be involuntary, it is inadmissible as evidence against the defendant, as its use would violate due process of law. The Supreme Court has established that coercive police activity can render a statement involuntary, and thus inadmissible, highlighting the importance of assessing the environment in which the statement was made to determine its voluntariness. 

Answer: (C)

evidence_store.text: To the extent that Williamson also argues that his statements were inadmissible as involuntary under the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment, the trial court also did not err in finding that they were voluntarily made. Due process voluntariness claims also are evaluated by considering the totality of the circumstances. See Welbon v. State, 301 Ga. 106, 109 (2), 799 S.E.2d 793 (2017). And Williamson cites no circumstances other than the law enforcement comments that he contends undermined the Miranda warnings in arguing that his statements should have been suppressed.

For confessions to be admissible, the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment requires that they be voiuntary. While voluntariness is a fact question that is assessed by looking at the totality of the circumstances, the duration and manner of the police interrogation here indicate that the can the result of actual coercion.

The due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution bars the admission of "any involuntary statement obtained by a law enforcement officer from a criminal suspect by coercion." ( People v. Neal (2003) 31 Cal.4th 63, 79, 1 Cal.Rptr.3d 650, 72 P.3d 280 ( Neal ).) So when the police obtain a suspect's statements "by 'techniques and methods offensive to due process' ... or under circumstances in which the suspect clearly had no opportunity to exercise 'a free and unconstrained will,' " the statements are inadmissible. ( Oregon v. Elstad (1985) 470 U.S. 298, 304, 105 S.Ct. 1285, 84 L.Ed.2d 222 ( Elstad ), citation omitted, quoting Haynes v. Washington (1963) 373 U.S. 503, 514, 83 S.Ct. 1336, 10 L.Ed.2d 513.)

"There are 'two constitutional bases for the requirement that a confession be voluntary to be admitted into evidence: the Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination and the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.' " State v. Ward , 2009 WI 60, ¶18, 318 Wis. 2d 301, 767 N.W.2d 236 (citations omitted). Admission of involuntary statements made during a criminal investigation violates a defendant's due process rights under the Fourteenth Amendment and article I, section 8 of the Wisconsin Constitution. State v. Hoppe , 2003 WI 43, ¶36, 261 Wis. 2d 294, 661 N.W.2d 407. "A defendant's statements are voluntary if they are the product of a free and unconstrained will, reflecting deliberateness of choice, as opposed to the result of a conspicuously unequal confrontation in which the pressures brought to bear on the defendant by representatives of the State exceeded the defendant's ability to resist." Id. (citing State v. Clappes , 136 Wis. 2d 222, 236, 401 N.W.2d 759 (1987) ). The absence of Miranda warnings is a consideration. See Hoppe , 261 Wis. 2d 294, ¶56. We look to the totality of the circumstances to determine whether a defendant's statements were voluntarily. Id. , ¶38.

¶20 The admission of involuntary statements violates a defendant's right to due process. State v. Hoppe , 2003 WI 43, ¶36, 261 Wis. 2d 294, 661 N.W.2d 407. Statements are voluntary if they arise from a free and unconstrained will and reflect deliberateness of choice, as opposed to arising from an unequal confrontation in which the pressures on the defendant exceed the defendant's ability to resist. Id. "Coercive or improper police conduct is a necessary prerequisite for a finding of involuntariness." Id. , ¶37.

question: Based upon the foregoing facts, if Paul sues Shampoo Company to recover damages for his dermatitis, his most promising theory of liability will be that the No-Flake shampoo
final_answer:
To determine the most promising theory of liability for Paul's claim against Shampoo Company, let's analyze the information provided and the options available.

1. **Manufacturing Defect (Option A):** A manufacturing defect refers to a flaw in the production process that makes the product deviate from its intended design. However, the information provided does not indicate that the shampoo itself was improperly manufactured or that there was a defect in its production. The focus is on the lack of warning about potential dangers, not on how the shampoo was made.

2. **Design Defect (Option B):** A design defect claim argues that the product's design is inherently flawed, making it unreasonably dangerous. While this could be a potential claim, the details given do not specifically address the design of the shampoo as being the issue. The concern seems to be more about the information provided to the user rather than the inherent design of the product.

3. **Inherently Dangerous (Option C):** This option suggests that the product is dangerous by its very nature. However, this does not directly address the issue at hand, which is the lack of adequate warning. A product can be considered inherently dangerous, but if proper warnings are provided, the manufacturer may not be liable for injuries resulting from its use.

4. **Inadequately Labeled to Warn of Its Dangers (Option D):** This option directly addresses the issue presented. The passages provided emphasize the importance of adequate warnings and instructions for the safe use of products. If a product poses certain risks, and these risks are not adequately communicated to the user, the manufacturer can be held liable for injuries resulting from the lack of warning. Given that Paul's dermatitis could have been caused by an ingredient in the shampoo and there was no adequate warning about this potential danger, this theory seems the most promising.

Based on the analysis, the most promising theory of liability for Paul's claim against Shampoo Company is that the No-Flake shampoo was inadequately labeled to warn of its dangers.

Answer: (D)

choices.A: had an unreasonably dangerous manufacturing defect.
choices.B: had an unreasonably dangerous design defect.
choices.C: was inherently dangerous.
choices.D: was inadequately labeled to warn of its dangers.

gold_passage: “In general, a supplier has a duty to warn end users of a dangerous product if it is reasonably foreseeable that an injury could occur in its use.” Gray v. Badger Mining Corp., 676 N.W.2d 268, 274 (Minn.2004). The duty to warn includes providing adequate instructions for the safe use of the product. Id. “[W]here the manufacturer or the seller of a product has actual or constructive knowledge of danger to users, the seller or manufacturer has a duty to give warning of such dangers.” Frey v. Montg
snap_answer:
To determine the most promising theory of liability for Paul's claim against Shampoo Company, let's analyze each option step by step:

(A) **Had an unreasonably dangerous manufacturing defect.** This theory would imply that the shampoo Paul used was not made according to the company's specifications, resulting in a product that was more dangerous than it was intended to be. However, without evidence that the shampoo was improperly manufactured (e.g., contamination, incorrect ingredients), this claim might be difficult to prove.

(B) **Had an unreasonably dangerous design defect.** This theory suggests that the design of the No-Flake shampoo itself is inherently flawed, making it unreasonably dangerous for its intended use. For Paul to succeed under this theory, he would need to show that a reasonable alternative design could have been implemented that would have prevented his dermatitis without significantly impairing the product's usefulness.

(C) **Was inherently dangerous.** This option is somewhat vague and not typically a standalone theory for product liability. Most products have some level of inherent danger if not used properly. This option does not directly address the specific issue of whether the shampoo was defective in its design, manufacture, or warning.

(D) **Was inadequately labeled to warn of its dangers.** This theory focuses on the idea that even if the shampoo was properly designed and manufactured, the company failed to provide adequate warnings about potential risks associated with its use, such as the risk of dermatitis. If Paul can show that his dermatitis was a foreseeable risk that the company should have warned about, and that the lack of adequate warning led to his injury, this could be a strong basis for liability.

Given the information provided, the most promising theory of liability seems to be related to the adequacy of the warnings provided with the product, as this directly addresses the issue of whether the company took sufficient steps to inform users of potential risks. This is particularly relevant if the dermatitis was a known or foreseeable side effect that the company should have warned against.

Answer: (D)
hyde_passage:
According to the principles of product liability law, a manufacturer has a duty to provide adequate warnings about the potential risks associated with the use of its products. A product is considered defective if it fails to include adequate warnings or instructions, and this defect causes harm to the user. The failure to warn of a known or foreseeable risk can give rise to liability, even if the product is properly designed and manufactured. 

Answer: (D)

evidence_store.text: In any product liability action the manufacturer or seller shall not be liable for harm caused by a failure to warn if the product contains an adequate warning or instruction or, in the case of dangers a manufacturer or seller discovers or reasonably should discover after the product leaves its control, if the manufacturer or seller provides an adequate warning or instruction. An adequate product warning or instruction is one that a reasonably prudent person in the same or similar circumstances would have provided with respect to the danger and that communicates adequate information on the dangers and safe use of the product, taking into account the characteristics of, and the ordinary knowledge common to, the persons by whom the product is intended to be used, or in the case of prescription drugs, taking into account the characteristics of, and the ordinary knowledge common to, the prescribing physician. If the warning or instruction given in connection with a drug or device or food or food additive has been approved or prescribed by the federal Food and Drug Administration under the "Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act," 52 Stat. 1040, 21 U.S.C. § 301 et seq. or the "Public Health Service Act," 58 Stat. 682, 42 U.S.C. § 201 et seq., a rebuttable presumption shall arise that the warning or instruction is adequate. For purposes of this section, the terms "drug", "device", "food", and "food additive" have the meanings defined in the "Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act."

A product is not reasonably safe because adequate warnings or instructions were not provided after the product was manufactured where a manufacturer learned or where a reasonably prudent manufacturer should have learned about a danger connected with the product after it was manufactured. In such a case, the manufacturer is under a duty to act with regard to issuing warnings or instructions concerning the danger in the manner that a reasonably prudent manufacturer would act in the same or similar circumstances. This duty is satisfied if the manufacturer exercises reasonable care to inform product users.

¶ 7 As indicated above, Plaintiff in this case asserts a defect existed in the form of an inadequate warning. "The manufacturer of a product has a duty to warn the consumer of potential dangers which may occur from the use of the product when it is known or should be known that hazards exist." Swift , ¶ 16 (quoting McKee v. Moore , 1982 OK 71, ¶ 4, 648 P.2d 21 ).

Because a manufacturer generally has a duty to warn consumers about the hazards of its product, a product may be dangerous because it lacks adequate warnings or instructions. Webb v. Special Electric Co. Inc., 63 Cal.4th 167, 180-81, 202 Cal.Rptr.3d 460, 370 P.3d 1022 (2016). Manufacturers are strictly liable for injuries caused by their failure to provide adequate warnings of known or reasonably scientifically knowable dangers at the time they manufactured and distributed their product. Johnson v. American Standard, Inc., 43 Cal.4th 56, 64, 74 Cal.Rptr.3d 108, 179 P.3d 905 (2008) ; Carlin v. Superior Ct., 13 Cal.4th 1104, 1108-09, 1112, 56 Cal.Rptr.2d 162, 920 P.2d 1347 (1996). A plausible claim for a failure to warn should include allegations that inter alia identify which danger was not warned against, explain that the danger was substantial, and that the danger was known or reasonably knowable, or explain how any warning that was given was inadequate. Lucas, 726 F.Supp.2d at 1156 n.1 ; Altman v. HO Sports Co., 2009 WL 4163512, *8-*9, 2009 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 108971, *23-*25 (E.D. Cal. Nov. 19, 2009) ; Johnson, 43 Cal.4th at 64-67, 74 Cal.Rptr.3d 108, 179 P.3d 905 ; Wright v. Stang Mfg. Co., 54 Cal.App.4th 1218, 1230, 63 Cal.Rptr.2d 422 (1997). However, "in the case of prescription drugs, the duty to warn runs to the physician, not the patient." Carlin, 13 Cal.4th at 1116, 56 Cal.Rptr.2d 162, 920 P.2d 1347 ; see T.H. v. Novartis Pharmaceuticals Corp., 4 Cal.5th 145, 164, 226 Cal.Rptr.3d 336, 407 P.3d 18 (2017) ; Bigler-Engler v. Breg, Inc., 7 Cal.App.5th 276, 319, 213 Cal.Rptr.3d 82 (2017). "If adequate warning of potential dangers of a drug has been given to doctors, there is no need by the drug manufacturer to insure that the warning reaches the doctor's patient for whom the drug is prescribed." Stevens v. Parke, Davis & Co, 9 Cal.3d 51, 65, 107 Cal.Rptr. 45, 507 P.2d 653 (1973) ; Bigler-Engler, 7 Cal.App.5th at 319, 213 Cal.Rptr.3d 82. Physicians typically rely on a prescription drug's warning label. T.H. v. Novartis Pharmaceuticals Corp., 4 Cal.5th 145, 178, 226 Cal.Rptr.3d 336, 407 P.3d 18 (2017). "Whether the warning is adequate is usually a question of fact." Schwoerer v. Union Oil Co., 14 Cal.App.4th 103, 112, 17 Cal.Rptr.2d 227 (1993). Finally, the failure to warn must be a substantial factor in causing the plaintiff's harm. See Ramos v. Brenntag Specialties, Inc., 63 Cal.4th 500, 509, 203 Cal.Rptr.3d 273, 372 P.3d 200 (2016) ; Huitt v. Southern Cal. Gas Co., 188 Cal.App.4th 1586, 1604, 116 Cal.Rptr.3d 453 (2010).

A products liability action based on strict liability requires: (i) a strict duty by a commercial supplier; (ii) production or sale of a defective product; (iii) actual and proximate causation; and (iv) damages. A product is legally defective if it was unreasonably dangerous and could be made safer by adequate warnings, even if it is not actually defective in that it apparently performed as it was meant to do. Even though professional users may have known of the danger, it was not obvious, and it could have been avoided at minimal cost by including a specific warning. To prove actual cause where the plaintiff's claim is that the product is defective because of lack of an adequate warning, the plaintiff is entitled to a presumption that an adequate warning would have been read and heeded.

question: In evaluating the constitutionality of this state statute under the supremacy clause, which of the following would be most directly relevant?
final_answer:
To evaluate the constitutionality of a state statute under the Supremacy Clause, we need to consider whether the state statute conflicts with federal law. The Supremacy Clause establishes that federal law is the "supreme Law of the Land" and takes precedence over state law when there is a conflict. 

Given the context of the question, which implies a state statute potentially conflicting with federal regulations or laws, the most directly relevant information would be related to federal laws or treaties that could supersede the state statute in question. 

Option (A), the general unemployment rate in the nation, does not directly relate to the conflict between federal and state law. Option (C), the need of the state for this particular statute, might be relevant in discussions about the rationale behind the state statute but does not directly address the Supremacy Clause's application. Option (D), the number of aliens currently residing in Yuma, could be relevant in certain contexts (such as immigration laws) but is not broadly applicable to evaluating the constitutionality of any state statute under the Supremacy Clause.

Option (B), the treaties and immigration laws of the United States, directly pertains to federal law, which is what the Supremacy Clause mandates should supersede conflicting state laws. Therefore, when evaluating the constitutionality of a state statute, comparing it against federal treaties and laws (such as immigration laws, if the state statute pertains to immigration) would be most directly relevant.

Answer: (B)

snap_answer:
To evaluate the constitutionality of a state statute under the supremacy clause, we need to consider whether the state statute conflicts with federal law. The supremacy clause, found in Article VI, Clause 2 of the United States Constitution, states that federal law is the supreme law of the land, and therefore, any state law that conflicts with federal law is preempted and thus unconstitutional.

Given the context, which involves the evaluation of a state statute, we should look for the option that directly relates to federal law or the potential conflict between state and federal law.

Option (A), "The general unemployment rate in the nation," does not directly relate to the constitutionality of a state statute under the supremacy clause, as it is an economic indicator rather than a legal principle or federal law.

Option (C), "The need of the state for this particular statute," might be relevant in discussions about the policy behind the statute but does not directly address the supremacy clause's requirement that state laws must not conflict with federal laws.

Option (D), "The number of aliens currently residing in Yuma," could be relevant to the impact or the context of the statute but does not directly address the question of whether the statute conflicts with federal law.

Option (B), "The treaties and immigration laws of the United States," directly relates to federal law, specifically in areas that are likely to be relevant to a state statute that could potentially conflict with federal authority, such as immigration. Since immigration is a matter primarily regulated by the federal government, any state statute attempting to regulate immigration or related matters could potentially conflict with federal treaties and laws, making this option most directly relevant to evaluating the constitutionality of the state statute under the supremacy clause.

Answer: (B)
hyde_passage: According to the principles of federal preemption, a state statute is deemed unconstitutional under the supremacy clause if it conflicts with federal law, including treaties and statutes enacted by Congress. The doctrine of preemption holds that federal law occupies the field of regulation in areas such as immigration, thereby preempting state laws that attempt to regulate the same field. In evaluating the constitutionality of a state statute under the supremacy clause, the court will consider whether the state statute is in conflict with federal treaties and laws, rendering option (B) the most directly relevant factor.

evidence_store.text: Under the Supremacy Clause, federal law may supersede, or preempt, state law in several different ways: (1) Congress may expressly state that federal law preempts state law (express preemption); (2) Congress' intent to preempt state law may be inferred from its comprehensive regulation of an area of law (field preemption); or (3) state law may actually conflict with the federal law (conflict preemption) - i.e., where compliance with both federal law and state law is impossible, or where the state law stands in the way of the accomplishment and execution of the purposes and objectives of Congress. Hillsborough, 471 U.S. at 713, 105 S.Ct. 2371 ; see also Gunter v. Farmers Ins. Co., Inc., 736 F.3d 768, 771 (8th Cir. 2013). Congress may evince its intent to pre-empt state law either implicitly or explicitly. Cipollone v. Liggett Grp., Inc., 505 U.S. 504, 516, 112 S.Ct. 2608, 120 L.Ed.2d 407 (1992). Federal regulations can have the same preemptive effect as federal law. Gunter, 736 F.3d at 771-72.

There are three classes of preemption: express preemption, conflict preemption, and field preemption. Pursuant to the Supremacy Clause, U.S. Const. art. VI, cl. 2, federal law preempts state law when "(1) Congress enacts a statute that explicitly preempts state law; (2) state law actually conflicts with federal law; or (3) federal law occupies a legislative field to such an extent that it is reasonable to conclude that Congress left no room for state regulation in that field." Chae v. SLM Corp. , 593 F.3d 936, 941 (9th Cir. 2010).

The Supreme Court has concluded that the Supremacy Clause of the United States Constitution provides the basis for Congress's power to preempt state law. This clause provides that federal law “shall be the supreme Law of the Land; and the Judges in every State shall be bound thereby, any Thing in the Constitution or Law of any State to the Contrary notwithstanding.” The Supremacy Clause provides the constitutional authority for the proposition that conflicts between federal and state law are resolved in favor of federal law.

The Supremacy Clause provides that “the Laws of the United States” (as well as treaties and the Constitution itself) “shall be the supreme Law of the Land ... any Thing in the Constitution or Laws of any state to the Contrary notwithstanding.” Art. VI, cl. 2. Congress may consequently pre-empt, i.e., invalidate, a state law through federal legislation. It may do so through express language in a statute. But even where, as here, a statute does not refer expressly to pre-emption, Congress may implicitly pre-empt a state law, rule, or other state action. See Sprietsma v. Mercury Marine, 537 U.S. 51, 64, 123 S.Ct. 518, 154 L.Ed.2d 466 (2002). It may do so either through “field” pre-emption or “conflict” pre-emption. [C]onflict pre-emption exists where “compliance with both state and federal law is impossible,” or where “the state law ‘stands as an obstacle to the accomplishment and execution of the full purposes and objectives of Congress.’ ” California v. ARC America Corp., 490 U.S. 93, 100, 101, 109 S.Ct. 1661, 104 L.Ed.2d 86 (1989). In either situation, federal law must prevail.

The Constitution limits state sovereignty "by granting certain legislative powers to Congress while providing in the Supremacy Clause that federal law is the 'supreme Law of the Land ... any Thing in the Constitution or Laws of any State to the Contrary notwithstanding.' " Murphy v. NCAA , --- U.S. ----, 138 S. Ct. 1461, 1476, 200 L.Ed.2d 854 (2018) (quoting U.S. Const. art. VI, cl. 2 ) (internal citation omitted). "This means that when federal and state law conflict, federal law prevails and state law is preempted." Id. The Supreme Court has "identified three different types of preemption-'conflict,' 'express,' and 'field,' but all of them work in the same way: Congress enacts a law that imposes restrictions or confers rights on private actors; a state law confers rights or imposes restrictions that conflict with the federal law; and therefore the federal law takes precedence and the state law is preempted." Id. at 1480 (internal citation omitted). For example, in Arizona v. United States , 567 U.S. 387, 132 S.Ct. 2492, 183 L.Ed.2d 351 (2012), the Court held that federal statutes "provide a full set of standards governing alien registration" and therefore "foreclose any state regulation in the area." Id. at 401, 132 S.Ct. 2492. In Murphy , the Court elaborated that "[w]hat this means is that the federal registration provisions not only impose federal registration obligations on aliens but also confer a federal right to be free from any other registration requirements." 138 S. Ct. at 1481. Authorized Federal regulations can preempt just as federal statutes can. See Hillsborough Cty. v. Automated Med. Labs., Inc. , 471 U.S. 707, 713, 105 S.Ct. 2371, 85 L.Ed.2d 714 (1985).
