evidence_store.text: "A party's status as a third-party beneficiary depends upon the intention of the contracting parties to benefit the third party, and this intention is determined by a construction of the contract as a whole." Am. Fletcher Mortg. Co. v. First Am. Inv. Corp., 463 F.Supp. 186, 195 (N.D. Ga. 1978). Additionally, the parties' intent must be identifiable on the face of the contract. Atcheson, 656 S.E.2d at 539. "[I]t must clearly appear from the contract that it was intended for [another's] benefit." Boller v. Robert W. Woodruff Arts Ctr., Inc., 311 Ga.App. 693, 716 S.E.2d 713, 717 (2011) (citations omitted). Importantly, "a third party is entitled to enforce only those specific provisions of a contract of which he is an intended beneficiary." Archer W. Contractors, Ltd. v. Estate of Pitts, 292 Ga. 219, 735 S.E.2d 772, 779 (2012) ; see also City of Atlanta v. Atl. Realty Co., 205 Ga.App. 1, 421 S.E.2d 113, 118 (1992) ("In appearing that Atlantic is an intended third party beneficiary to that paragraph 16 of the construction contract, Atlantic has the requisite standing to sue Banks, the promisor of that provision , for any breach of that provision , pursuant to OCGA § 9-2-20(b)." (emphasis added) ).

A contract that benefits a third party may confer rights on that party if he is an intended beneficiary rather than merely an incidental beneficiary. However, an intended beneficiary can enforce the contract only after his rights have vested. Vesting will occur when the beneficiary (i) manifests assent to the promise in a manner invited or requested by the parties, (ii) brings suit to enforce the promise, or (iii) materially changes position in justifiable reliance on the promise.

Courts generally look to a number of factors in determining whether a third party is an intended beneficiary or an incidental beneficiary, and are more likely to find intended beneficiary status when: (i) the third party is expressly designated in the contract; (ii) performance is to be made directly to the third party; (iii) the third party has any rights under the contract; and (iv) the third party stands in such a relationship to the promisee that one could infer that the promisee wished to make an agreement for the third party's benefit. If the court determines that a third party is an intended beneficiary, it must then decide whether his rights have vested, which is necessary to enforce the contract. Vesting occurs when the beneficiary: (i) manifests assent to the promise in a manner invited or requested by the parties; (ii) brings suit to enforce the promise; or (iii) materially changes position in justifiable reliance on the promise. The general rule regarding the vesting of rights in a third party applies to a donee beneficiary (i.e., a third party in a contract in which the promisee's primary intent in contracting is to confer a gift on the third party) as well as to a creditor beneficiary, so long as he is an intended beneficiary to the contract.

A person or entity is a third-party beneficiary to a contract, even without signing it, when (1) there "clearly appear[s] a promissory intent to benefit the third party" and (2) "the third party's reliance thereon [wa]s foreseeable." Lipshie v. Tracy Inv. Co., 93 Nev. 370, 379, 566 P.2d 819, 824-25 (1977) ; cf. Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 302 (Am. Law Inst. 1979) (stating that third-party beneficiary status exists where (1) the recognition of the beneficiary's right to performance is "appropriate to effectuate the intention of the parties [to a contact]," and (2) either the performance "will satisfy an obligation of the promisee to pay money to the beneficiary" or "the circumstances indicate that the promisee intends to give the beneficiary the benefit of the promised performance"). Whether a party is an intended third-party beneficiary "depends on the parties' intent, gleaned from reading the contract as a whole in light of the circumstances under which it was entered." Canfora v. Coast Hotels & Casinos, Inc., 121 Nev. 771, 779, 121 P.3d 599, 605 (2005) (internal quotation marks omitted).

Third-party beneficiary status is an exception to the privity requirement that applies in situations where the contract reflects the express or implied intention of the parties to benefit the third-party. State of Montana v. United States, 124 F.3d 1269, 1273 (Fed.Cir.1997). This analysis is commonly referred to as the “intention-to-benefit test.” Schuerman v. United States, 30 Fed.Cl. 420, 433 (1994). “Any such intent is found through an examination of the parties' entire contractual relationship ... and is ascertained by determining whether the beneficiary would be reasonable in relying on the promise as intending to confer a right on him.” Sallee v. United States, 41 Fed.Cl. 509, 514 (1998) (citing Schuerman, 30 Fed.Cl. at 433 and Montana, 124 F.3d at 1273). The intended beneficiary “need not be specifically or individually identified in the contract, but must fall within a class that the contract clearly intends to benefit.” Id. It is important, however, for the court to carefully “distinguish between incidental and indirect beneficiaries and direct beneficiaries, only the latter of which qualifies for third-party beneficiary status.” Schuerman, 30 Fed.Cl. at 433.

label: qa_EVIDENCE_mbe_1072
subject: EVIDENCE
question: Plaintiff sued Defendant under an age discrimination statute, alleging that Defendant refused to hire Plaintiff because she was over age 65. Defendant's defense was that he refused to employ Plaintiff because he reasonably believed that she would be unable to perform the job. Defendant seeks to testify that Employer, Plaintiff's former employer, advised him not to hire Plaintiff because she was unable to perform productively for more than four hours a day. The testimony of Defendant is
final_answer:
To determine the admissibility of the testimony of Defendant regarding Employer's advice not to hire Plaintiff, we must analyze the statement in the context of the rules of evidence, specifically focusing on hearsay and its exceptions.

1. **Definition of Hearsay**: Hearsay is defined as a statement that the declarant does not make while testifying at the current trial or hearing, and a party offers in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted in the statement (Fed. R. Evid. 801(c)). 

2. **Employer's Statement as Hearsay**: Employer's statement to Defendant that Plaintiff was unable to perform productively for more than four hours a day is an out-of-court statement offered to prove the truth of the matter asserted (i.e., Plaintiff's productivity). Thus, it qualifies as hearsay.

3. **Exceptions to Hearsay**: For hearsay to be admissible, it must fall under one of the exceptions to the hearsay rule. One such exception is when a statement is offered to prove the state of mind of the person it is being used against or, in some contexts, the state of mind of the declarant or another person (Miss. R. Evid. 803(3), as discussed in Source 4).

4. **State of Mind Exception**: The state of mind exception allows for the admission of hearsay statements when they are offered to prove the declarant's or another person's state of mind, rather than the truth of the matter asserted. In this case, Defendant seeks to introduce Employer's statement to show Defendant's reason for not hiring Plaintiff, which relates to Defendant's state of mind (his belief about Plaintiff's abilities).

5. **Application to the Scenario**: Defendant's testimony about Employer's advice is being offered to show why Defendant refused to hire Plaintiff, which is to prove Defendant's state of mind (his reason or belief) at the time of the decision. This use does not aim to prove the truth of Employer's statement about Plaintiff's productivity but rather to demonstrate Defendant's thought process and decision-making basis.

6. **Conclusion**: Given that the statement is being used to demonstrate Defendant's state of mind (his reason for refusing to hire Plaintiff), it falls under the state of mind exception to the hearsay rule. Therefore, the testimony of Defendant regarding Employer's advice is admissible as evidence of Defendant's reason for refusing to hire Plaintiff.

Answer: (D)

choices.A: inadmissible, because Defendant's opinion of Plaintiff's abilities is not based on personal knowledge.
choices.B: inadmissible, because Employer's statement is hearsay not within any exception.
choices.C: admissible as evidence that Plaintiff would be unable to work longer than four hours per day.
choices.D: admissible as evidence of Defendant's reason for refusing to hire Plaintiff.

First, let's consider if the testimony is being offered to prove the truth of the matter asserted (i.e., that Plaintiff was unable to perform productively for more than four hours a day). Hearsay is generally inadmissible unless it falls under one of the exceptions. However, in this scenario, the statement by Employer is not being offered to prove Plaintiff's ability or inability to work but rather to explain why Defendant made the decision he did.

The key here is the purpose for which the evidence is being offered. If it's to show Defendant's state of mind (his reason for not hiring Plaintiff), then it's not being offered for the truth of the matter asserted but rather to understand Defendant's thought process and decision-making. This distinction is crucial because statements that are offered to provide context or motivation for a person's actions can be admissible, even if they contain hearsay, under the theory that they are not being used to prove the truth of the matter asserted but to show the effect on the listener (in this case, Defendant).

Given this, let's evaluate the options:

(A) This option suggests the testimony is inadmissible because Defendant's opinion of Plaintiff's abilities is not based on personal knowledge. However, the testimony is not about Defendant's personal opinion of Plaintiff's abilities but about what Employer told him.

While Employer's statement could be considered hearsay if offered for the truth of the matter asserted, the context here is about Defendant's reason for not hiring Plaintiff, not directly about proving Plaintiff's work capability.

(C) This option suggests the testimony is admissible as evidence that Plaintiff would be unable to work longer than four hours per day. This would be incorrect because, in this context, the testimony is not being used to prove Plaintiff's work capability directly but to explain Defendant's decision-making process.

(D) This option states the testimony is admissible as evidence of Defendant's reason for refusing to hire Plaintiff. This aligns with the analysis that the testimony is being used to understand Defendant's state of mind and decision-making process, not to prove the truth of the matter asserted about Plaintiff's work abilities.

Answer: (D)

evidence_store.text: Hearsay is "a statement that (1) the declarant does not make while testifying at the current trial or hearing; and (2) a party offers in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted in the statement." Pa.R.E. 801(c). It is not admissible as evidence unless an exception to the hearsay rule applies. See Pa.R.E. 802 ; see also Pa.R.E. 802, 803, 803.1, and 804. One of the exceptions to the rule against hearsay is the state of mind exception:

"Hearsay is not admissible unless [a federal statute, rule of evidence, or other rule prescribed by the Supreme Court] provides otherwise ...." Fed. R. Evid. 802. Federal Rule of Evidence 801 defines hearsay as "a statement that: (1) the declarant does not make while testifying at the current trial or hearing; and (2) a party offers in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted in the statement." Fed. R. Evid. 801(c). The Rule excludes from this definition several categories of statements, including statements "offered against an opposing party" that were "made by the party's agent or employee on a matter within the scope of that relationship and while it existed." Fed. R. Evid. 801(d)(2)(D).

The district court has this one right. L.H. surely knew something about the processes she was involved in. And she might have even known something about how the final decisions were made. But once she was excluded from the decisionmaking process in January, Weil had to offer some basis for her statement. Otherwise, the statement may be nothing more than her uninformed opinion, a statement "made in [that employee's] capacity as wiseacre only." Fairchild v. All Am. Check Cashing, Inc. , 815 F.3d 959, 967 (5th Cir. 2016) (quoting Staheli v. Univ. of Miss. , 854 F.2d 121, 127 (5th Cir. 1988) ). Federal Rule of Evidence 801(d)(2)(D) is not only an exclusion from the hearsay rule, it is a powerful rule of attribution: If admitted, L.H.'s statement becomes Frontier's statement, as L.H. is presumed to be speaking on behalf of her employer.

¶44. Under the applicable exception, Mississippi Rules of Evidence 803(3), statements "offered to prove the defendant's state of mind, rather than the truth of [the matters] assert[ed] .... [are] not hearsay under the Mississippi Rules of Evidence." Peterson v. State , 671 So.2d 647, 657 (Miss. 1996), superseded by rule on other grounds as recognized in Caston v. State , 949 So.2d 852, 855-56 (Miss. Ct. App. 2007). See generally Eselin-Bullock & Assocs. Ins. Agency, Inc. v. Nat'l Gen. Ins. Co. , 604 So.2d 236, 242 (Miss. 1992). See also United States v. Detrich , 865 F.2d 17, 21 (2d Cir. 1988) (when "the statement is offered as circumstantial evidence of [the defendant's] state of mind, it does not fall within the definition" of hearsay); Lambert v. State , 888 P.2d 494, 501 (Okla. Crim. App. 1994) ("The record also contains testimony from Dr. Goodman that his conversations with Hain were relied upon in determining [the defendant's] competency, and evaluating his mental state. There is no assertion that the information related by Goodman was offered to prove the truth of the matter asserted. Rather, the statement was used to evaluate [the defendant's] state of mind ...."); State v. Crafts , 226 Conn. 237, 627 A.2d 877, 886 (1993) ("A statement that is offered to establish circumstantially the state of mind of the declarant is not offered for the truth of the statement.").

Under Federal Rule of Evidence 801(d)(2)(D), a statement is not hearsay and may be admitted against an opposing party if the statement "was made by the party's agent or employee on a matter within the scope of that relationship and while it existed." The Rule sets forth three elements necessary for admitting a statement that would otherwise be excluded as hearsay: (1) the statement must be made by an agent or employee of the party against whom the statement is being offered; (2) the statement must concern a matter within the scope of that employment relationship; and (3) the statement must be made while the declarant is yet employed by the party. There is no additional requirement that the declarant must still be in the same scope of employment at the moment the statement is made. The Rule's language is unambiguous. The second element requires that the statement concern a matter that was at some time within the scope of the declarant's employment. The third element requires only that the statement be made while the declarant is yet employed; it does not require that the declarant still be in the same position that resulted in the matter being within the scope of the employment relationship.

label: qa_nan_mbe_99
subject: nan
question: Assume that Green paid the $2,500 on March 15 and that Brown completed the boathouse according to specifications, but that Green then refused to pay the second installment and repudiated the contract. Assume further that the absence of a writing is not raised as a defense. Which of the following is (are) correct? I. Brown has a cause of action against Green and his damages will be $2,500 II. Brown can refuse to dig the channel and not be liable for breach of contract
final_answer:
To answer this question, let's analyze the given information and the principles of anticipatory repudiation as outlined in the retrieved passages.

1. **Anticipatory Repudiation**: This doctrine allows a party to a contract to treat the contract as breached when the other party distinctly, unequivocally, and absolutely refuses to perform according to the terms of the agreement before the time for performance is due. This refusal must be clear and absolute, not just an expression of inability or a conditional statement.

2. **Scenario Given**: Green paid the first installment of $2,500 on March 15 and Brown completed the boathouse as specified. However, Green then refused to pay the second installment and repudiated the contract. This action by Green constitutes an anticipatory repudiation because he has clearly and unequivocally stated his intention not to fulfill his contractual obligations (paying the second installment) before the time for that performance is due.

3. **Brown's Position**:
   - **Cause of Action Against Green (I)**: Given Green's anticipatory repudiation, Brown has the right to consider the contract breached. According to the principles outlined, when one party repudiates a contract, the other party can treat this repudiation as a breach and seek remedies for breach, even before the performance was due. Therefore, Brown can seek damages for the breach. However, the calculation of damages ($2,500) would depend on the specifics of the contract and the losses incurred by Brown due to Green's repudiation. If the $2,500 represents the expected profit or loss directly resulting from Green's breach, then Brown might have a claim for this amount. Thus, statement I could be correct depending on the specifics of the damages calculation.
   - **Refusal to Dig the Channel (II)**: Since Green has repudiated the contract, Brown is no longer obligated to perform his part of the contract, which includes digging the channel. The doctrine of anticipatory repudiation allows the non-repudiating party (Brown, in this case) to suspend his own performance upon the repudiation by the other party. Therefore, Brown can refuse to dig the channel without being liable for breach of contract because Green's repudiation has already breached the contract. Thus, statement II is correct.

Given the analysis, both statements I and II are correct under the doctrine of anticipatory repudiation. Brown has a cause of action against Green for the breach (the specifics of damages would depend on the contract and losses incurred), and Brown can refuse to dig the channel without being liable for breach due to Green's repudiation of the contract.

Answer: (C)

choices.A: I only
choices.B: II only
choices.C: Both I and II
choices.D: Neither I nor II

gold_passage: When one party repudiates a contract, the innocent party may treat the repudiation as a breach or continue to perform under the contract and await the time of the agreed-upon performance. Ingersoll–Rand Co. v. Valero Energy Corp., 997 S.W.2d 203, 211 (Tex.1999).
snap_answer:
When Green repudiated the contract and refused to pay the second installment, Brown's obligation to perform his remaining duties under the contract (i.e., digging the channel) was excused due to Green's material breach. This is because a party's material breach of contract excuses the other party's performance.

Regarding statement I, when a party breaches a contract by repudiating it, the non-breaching party is entitled to recover damages. The amount of damages recoverable is typically the expectation interest, which aims to put the non-breaching party in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed. However, in this scenario, since Brown has already completed the boathouse according to specifications and received the first payment of $2,500, his damages for Green's repudiation would not include the $2,500 he has already received. Instead, Brown's damages would likely be the unpaid balance of the contract price (the second installment), minus any expenses Brown saved by not having to dig the channel.

Regarding statement II, because Green repudiated the contract, Brown is indeed justified in refusing to dig the channel. A party is not required to perform their obligations under a contract when the other party has materially breached or repudiated the contract.

Therefore, statement II is correct because Brown can refuse to dig the channel without being liable for breach of contract. Statement I, as explained, does not accurately reflect the damages Brown would be entitled to, given that he has already received $2,500 and his damages would relate to the loss of the second installment, not the recovery of the first.

Answer: (B)
hyde_passage:
According to the doctrine of anticipatory repudiation, when a party repudiates a contract, the non-breaching party is excused from further performance and may recover damages for the breach. The non-breaching party's damages are typically measured by the expectation interest, which aims to put the party in the position they would have been in had the contract been performed, minus any expenses saved by not having to perform. A material breach or repudiation by one party excuses the other party's performance, allowing them to refuse to fulfill their obligations under the contract without being liable for breach. 

Answer: (B)

evidence_store.text: {14} UJI 13-822 and UJI 13-824 NMRA address the doctrine of anticipatory repudiation. UJI 13-822 states that "[a] person may breach a contract by ... announcing ahead of time that he or she will not perform a contractual obligation when the time for that performance comes due[.]" And UJI 13-824 provides that "[i]t is a breach of contract if, before performance became due, [the promisor] announce[s] or otherwise demonstrate[s the promisor's] intention not to perform a contractual obligation[.]" Neither these instructions nor other authorities addressing anticipatory repudiation support Defendant's requested holding. The doctrine of anticipatory repudiation is an exception to the general rule that only a party's failure to perform as promised constitutes a breach. It allows a party to a contract to treat the contract as breached upon learning of the other party's repudiation of its contractual obligations-its "distinct, unequivocal, and absolute refusal to perform according to the terms of the agreement." Gilmore v. Duderstadt , 1998-NMCA-086, ¶ 15, 125 N.M. 330, 961 P.2d 175 (internal quotation marks and citation omitted). The doctrine protects a non-breaching party's expectation, not only that the other party will perform, but also that the other party will refrain from "substantially ... impair[ing]" that expectation of performance by allowing the non-repudiating party to file suit immediately after communication of the repudiation. Restatement (Second) of Contracts ch. 10, topic 3, intro. note (1981). See generally Murray, supra , §§ 54.1, 54.2.

anticipatory breach: In contract law, anticipatory breach occurs when a party repudiates prior to the date that the performance is due. Anticipatory breach is an excuse for non-performance by the non-breaching party. A party can retract its anticipatory breach provided that the non-breaching party has not relied on it.

§ 2-610. Anticipatory Repudiation. When either party repudiates the contractwith respect to a performance not yet due the loss of which will substantially impair the value of the contract to the other, the aggrieved party may (a) for a commercially reasonable time await performance by the repudiating party; or (b) resort to any remedy for breach (Section 2-703 or Section 2-711), even though he has notified the repudiating party that he would await the latter's performance and has urged retraction; and (c) in either case suspend his own performance or proceed in accordance with the provisions of this Article on the seller's right to identify goods to the contract notwithstanding breach or to salvage unfinished goods (Section 2-704).

[T]he anticipatory repudiation of a contract occurs when one party thereto repudiates his contractual obligation to perform prior to the time such performance is required under the terms of the contract. While technically such a repudiation is not a breach of contract, the contractual time for performance not having arrived, the law recognizes that under certain circumstances the innocent party to the contract may treat such an anticipatory repudiation as a breach thereof. Thus when one party to a bilateral contract of mutual dependent promises absolutely refuses to perform and repudiates the contract prior to the time of his performance, the innocent party is at liberty to consider himself absolved from any future performance on his part[.]

Accordingly, basic contract principles apply unless precluded by law. Id. One such principle is the doctrine of anticipatory repudiation.  Under that doctrine, When one party to [a] ... contract absolutely refuses to perform his contract, and before the time arrives for performance distinctly and unqualifiedly communicates that refusal to the other party, that other party can, if he choose, treat that refusal as a breach and commence an action at once therefor. United States v. Dekonty Corp., 922 F.2d 826, 828 (Fed.Cir.1991) (quoting Dingley v. Oler, 117 U.S. 490, 499–500, 6 S.Ct. 850, 29 L.Ed. 984 (1886)).  However, as stated by the Supreme Court: a mere assertion that the party will be unable, or will refuse to perform his contract, is not sufficient; it must be a distinct unequivocal absolute refusal to perform the promise, and must be treated and acted upon as such by the party to whom the promise was made.... Dingley, 117 U.S. at 503, 6 S.Ct. at 854 (quoting In re Smoot, 82 U.S. (15 Wall.) 36, 21 L.Ed. 107 (1872)).

label: qa_nan_mbe_361
subject: nan
question: Federal taxpayer Allen challenges the provision that allows the distribution of free textbooks to students in a private school where religious instruction is included in the curriculum. On the question of the adequacy of Allen's standing to raise the constitutional question, the most likely result is that standing will be
final_answer:
To determine the most likely result regarding Allen's standing to raise the constitutional question, we must analyze the criteria for taxpayer standing as established by the Supreme Court in Flast v. Cohen. According to Flast, a federal taxpayer can establish standing to challenge a congressional expenditure if two elements are met:

1. The taxpayer must challenge the exercise of congressional power under the taxing and spending clause of Article I, Section 8, of the Constitution.
2. The taxpayer must show that the challenged enactment exceeds specific constitutional limitations imposed upon the exercise of the congressional taxing and spending power.

In Allen's case, the challenge is to the provision allowing the distribution of free textbooks to students in a private school where religious instruction is included in the curriculum. This scenario involves a congressional spending authorization that could be seen as violating the Establishment Clause of the First Amendment, which is a specific constitutional limitation on the exercise of congressional spending power.

Given these considerations:

- Option (A) is incorrect because it overstates the breadth of taxpayer standing. Not any congressional spending authorization can be challenged by any taxpayer; the challenge must be based on a violation of specific constitutional limitations.
- Option (D) is incorrect because the issue of state action is not directly relevant to the question of taxpayer standing in challenges to federal expenditures. The focus is on whether the federal expenditure violates a specific constitutional limitation, such as the Establishment Clause.
- Option (C) might seem plausible because the concept of "nexus" is important in standing doctrine. However, in the context of taxpayer standing as defined by Flast v. Cohen, the required nexus is established if the taxpayer challenges a spending measure that violates a specific constitutional limitation, which is the case here.
- Option (B) is the most accurate. It reflects the Flast v. Cohen criteria for taxpayer standing, which Allen's challenge appears to meet: the challenge is to an exercise of congressional spending power, and it is based on a claimed violation of a specific constitutional limitation (the Establishment Clause).

Therefore, based on the criteria established in Flast v. Cohen, Allen's standing to raise the constitutional question would most likely be sustained because the challenge is to an exercise of congressional spending power based on a claimed violation of a specific constitutional limitation.

Answer: (B)

choices.A: sustained, because any congressional spending authorization can be challenged by any taxpayer
choices.B: sustained, because the challenge to the exercise of congressional spending power is based on a claimed violation of specific constitutional limitations on the exercise of such power
choices.C: denied, because there is insufficient nexus between the taxpayer and the challenged expenditures
choices.D: denied, because, in the case of private schools, no state action is involved

gold_passage: The spending power is of course not unlimited, Pennhurst State School and Hospital v. Halderman, 451 U.S. 1, 17, and n. 13, 101 S.Ct. 1531, 1540 n. 13, 67 L.Ed.2d 694 (1981), but is instead subject to several general restrictions articulated in our cases. The first of these limitations is derived from the language of the Constitution itself: the exercise of the spending power must be in pursuit of “the general welfare.” See Helvering v. Davis, 301 U.S. 619, 640–641, 57 S.Ct. 904, 908–909, 81 L.E
snap_answer:
To determine whether Allen has standing to challenge the provision, we need to analyze the concept of standing in the context of taxpayer suits. Standing is a legal term that refers to the right of a party to bring a lawsuit. In the case of taxpayer suits, the Supreme Court has established that a taxpayer has standing to challenge a congressional spending authorization if the taxpayer can show that the challenged expenditure violates a specific constitutional limitation on the exercise of congressional spending power.

In this case, Allen is challenging the provision that allows the distribution of free textbooks to students in a private school where religious instruction is included in the curriculum. The challenge is based on the claimed violation of the Establishment Clause of the First Amendment, which prohibits the government from making any law respecting an establishment of religion.

The key issue here is whether Allen, as a federal taxpayer, has standing to raise this constitutional question. The Supreme Court has held that a taxpayer has standing to challenge a congressional spending authorization if the challenge is based on a claimed violation of a specific constitutional limitation on the exercise of congressional spending power (Flast v. Cohen, 392 U.S. 83 (1968)).

Applying this principle to the present case, we can conclude that Allen's standing will likely be sustained because the challenge to the exercise of congressional spending power is based on a claimed violation of a specific constitutional limitation on the exercise of such power, namely the Establishment Clause.

Answer: (B)
hyde_passage: According to the doctrine established in Flast v. Cohen, 392 U.S. 83 (1968), a federal taxpayer has standing to challenge a congressional spending authorization if the taxpayer can show that the challenged expenditure violates a specific constitutional limitation on the exercise of congressional spending power. This rule allows taxpayers to bring suit when they allege that a particular expenditure is contrary to a specific constitutional provision, such as the Establishment Clause. As a result, a taxpayer's standing will be sustained when the challenge is based on a claimed violation of a specific constitutional limitation on congressional spending power, as is the case with Allen's challenge to the distribution of free textbooks to private schools with religious instruction.

evidence_store.text: The Supreme Court cracked the door open to taxpayer standing in 1968, with its decision in Flast v. Cohen , 392 U.S. 83, 88, 88 S.Ct. 1942, 20 L.Ed.2d 947 (1968). It permitted the plaintiffs in that case to challenge the expenditure of federal funds used for the "instruction ... and for guidance in religious and sectarian schools" and the "purchase of textbooks and instructional and library materials for use in religious and sectarian schools" as a violation of the Establishment Clause. Id. at 87, 88 S.Ct. 1942. The Court observed that "[t]he nexus demanded of federal taxpayers" to establish standing consists of two elements. Id. at 102, 88 S.Ct. 1942. First, federal taxpayers may challenge "the unconstitutionality only of exercises of congressional power under the taxing and spending clause of Art. I, s 8, of the Constitution." Id. Second, taxpayers "must show that the challenged enactment exceeds specific constitutional limitations imposed upon the exercise of the congressional taxing and spending power." Id. at 102-03, 88 S.Ct. 1942. Thus, the Court explained, a plaintiff could establish taxpayer standing only if challenging a "congressional action under the taxing and spending clause [alleged to be] in derogation of those constitutional provisions which operate to restrict the exercise of the taxing and spending power," such as the Establishment Clause. Id. at 106, 88 S.Ct. 1942. Applying that standard, the Court found that the plaintiffs in Flast qualified for taxpayer standing.

Flast v. Cohen created a narrow exception to the general rule of standing that permits taxpayers to challenge the expenditure of federal funds in religious schools under the express limitations of the First Amendment Establishment Clause. 392 U.S. 83, 103, 88 S.Ct. 1942, 20 L.Ed.2d 947 (1968).

To raise a constitutional challenge to a government action, the person who is challenging it must have standing to raise the constitutional issue. To have standing, a person must be able to assert that he is injured by a government program. This injury must be more than the merely theoretical injury that all persons suffer by seeing their government engage in unconstitutional actions. In cases where a taxpayer is claiming injury from the use of his taxes for an unconstitutional appropriation or expenditure of public funds, the Supreme Court has required the taxpayer to show that the challenged measure (i) was enacted under the governmental body's power to tax and spend (rather than as an incidental expenditure in the administration of an essentially regulatory statute), and (ii) exceeds some specific limitation on that power. The only specific limitation that the Court has recognized when an appropriation measure has been challenged is the First Amendment's Establishment Clause. [See Flast v. Cohen (1968)] The Establishment Clause, which is applicable to the states through the Fourteenth Amendment, requires that government programs (i) must have a secular purpose, (ii) must have a primary effect that neither advances nor inhibits religion, and (iii) must not produce excessive government entanglement with religion. While the factor of the fraction of taxes used for the appropriation being minimal has been relied on by the Supreme Court in its general refusal to grant federal and state taxpayers standing, it has not been used to deny municipal taxpayers standing in federal courts. [See Asarco, Inc. v. Kadish (1989)] Furthermore, the Court has carved out a narrow exception for federal taxpayers to challenge an appropriation, despite the fact that any one taxpayer's contribution would be minute and indeterminable, where the measure was enacted under Congress's taxing and spending powers and violates the Establishment Clause. Generally taxpayers do not have standing to sue on constitutional grounds. The Supreme Court has not expanded the standing rules beyond what Flast v. Cohen had established.

Defendants rely on Harrington v. Schlesinger , 528 F.2d 455 (4th Cir. 1975), in which the Fourth Circuit held that the claims of several individual taxpayers who alleged that the government was spending money in violation of two statutes did not satisfy the test for taxpayer standing enunciated in Flast v. Cohen , 392 U.S. 83, 88 S.Ct. 1942, 20 L.Ed.2d 947 (1968), because they "present[ed] no constitutional challenge to any congressional appropriation," Harrington , 528 F.2d at 457. Harrington is largely inapposite, because Plaintiffs do not rely on taxpayer standing here. The court in Harrington noted, however, that "[i]f there were a clear and flagrant violation of congressional limitations upon expenditures, a court in a taxpayer suit might find its intervention appropriate." Id. at 458. Thus, if Harrington has any persuasive value here, we think it is in suggesting that Plaintiffs do have a cause of action because, as we have discussed, there has been a clear violation of Congress' limits on expenditures.

Finally, Plaintiffs make a policy argument, asserting that if taxpayer standing is denied here, "Establishment Clause violations uniquely could go unchecked in many instances." Pls.' Opp'n at 21. But the Court rejected that very argument in Hein . See 551 U.S. at 614, 127 S.Ct. 2553 (rejecting the "parade of horribles that [respondents] claim could occur if Flast is not extended to discretionary Executive Branch expenditures"). Hein makes clear that taxpayer standing is not an all-access pass to the federal courts to challenge Executive Branch expenditures that might run afoul of the Establishment Clause. See Hein , 551 U.S. at 615, 127 S.Ct. 2553 (refusing to "expand[ ] [ Flast ] to the limit of its logic"). Other means are available to curtail Executive Branch encroachment of the Establishment Clause, including Congressional action and, as will be seen, challenges "by plaintiffs who would possess standing based on grounds other than taxpayer standing." Id. Thus, Plaintiffs assertion that, absent recognition of taxpayer standing in this case, Executive Branch abuses might go unchecked is unpersuasive.

label: qa_CONST. LAW_mbe_1041
subject: CONST. LAW
question: The state of Brunswick enacted a statute providing for the closure of the official state records of arrest and prosecution of all persons acquitted of a crime by a court or against whom criminal charges were filed and subsequently dropped or dismissed. The purpose of this statute is to protect these persons from further publicity or embarrassment relating to those state proceedings. However, this statute does not prohibit the publication of such information that is in the possession of private persons. A prominent businessman in Neosho City in Brunswick was arrested and charged with rape. Prior to trial, the prosecutor announced that new information indicated that the charges should be dropped. He then dropped the charges without further explanation, and the records relating thereto were closed to the public pursuant to the Brunswick statute. The Neosho City Times conducted an investigation to determine why the businessman was not prosecuted, but was refused access to the closed official state records. In an effort to determine whether the law enforcement agencies involved were properly doing their duty, the Times filed suit against appropriate state officials to force opening of the records and to invalidate the statute on constitutional grounds. Which of the following would be most helpful to the state in defending the constitutionality of this statute?
final_answer:
To defend the constitutionality of the statute, the state needs to demonstrate that the closure of the records is justified by a compelling government interest and that the statute is narrowly tailored to serve that interest. 

Option (A) suggests that the statute treats all persons in similar situations equally, which could be relevant in an equal protection analysis but is not directly relevant to the question of whether the statute violates the First Amendment right of access to judicial records.

Option (B) is a correct statement of the law regarding the rights of the press versus those of the general public, but it does not directly address why the statute's closure of records would be constitutional.

Option (D) is incorrect because while the state has significant discretion over its own records, the First Amendment can limit this discretion, particularly when the records are of judicial proceedings.

Option (C) is the most helpful to the state in defending the constitutionality of the statute. This option highlights that the statute does not completely prevent the dissemination of the information contained in the records; it only limits access to the official records themselves. This distinction could be crucial in arguing that the statute is narrowly tailored to protect the privacy and reputation of individuals who have been acquitted or had charges dropped, without unnecessarily restricting the public's or the press's ability to access and report on the information. This could help in demonstrating that the statute strikes a balance between the individual's privacy interests and the public's right to know, potentially justifying the limitation on access to the records under the First Amendment.

Answer: (C)

choices.A: The fact that the statute treats in an identical manner the arrest and prosecution records of all persons who have been acquitted of a crime by a court or against whom criminal charges were filed and subsequently dropped or dismissed.
choices.B: The argument that the rights of the press are no greater than those of citizens generally.
choices.C: The fact that the statute only prohibits public access to these official state records and does not prohibit the publication of information they contain that is in the possession of private persons.
choices.D: The argument that the state may seal official records owned by the state on any basis its legislature chooses.

A corollary of the right to publish must be the right to gather news. The full flow of information to the public protected by the free-press guarantee would be severely curtailed if no protection whatever were afforded to the process by which news is assembled and dis
snap_answer:
To defend the constitutionality of the Brunswick statute, the state needs to address the potential First Amendment concerns raised by the Neosho City Times. The Times is essentially arguing that the statute violates their freedom of the press by denying them access to information that could be of public interest. The state's defense should focus on demonstrating that the statute is narrowly tailored to serve a significant government interest and that it does not impermissibly restrict the freedom of the press.

Option (A) discusses the equal treatment of individuals under the statute, which is relevant to equal protection analysis but does not directly address the First Amendment concerns at issue here.

Option (B) is a correct statement of law regarding the rights of the press, as the Supreme Court has held that the press has no greater right of access to information than the general public. However, this argument alone may not be sufficient to defend the statute, as the issue is not just about the rights of the press versus those of the public, but also about the government's ability to restrict access to information.

Option (D) suggests an absolute right by the state to seal records, which is not accurate. The government cannot seal records simply on any basis it chooses, especially if doing so infringes on constitutional rights such as freedom of the press.

Option (C) is the most helpful to the state in defending the constitutionality of the statute. By pointing out that the statute only prohibits public access to official state records and does not prohibit the publication of information those records contain that is in the possession of private persons, the state can argue that the statute is narrowly tailored to protect the privacy interests of individuals who have been acquitted or had charges dropped, without unnecessarily restricting the flow of information to the public. This distinction is crucial because it shows that the state is not attempting to censor information altogether but rather to control access to certain official records, which could be seen as a less restrictive means of achieving the government's interest in protecting privacy.

Answer: (C)
hyde_passage:
According to established First Amendment jurisprudence, a state may restrict access to certain government records if the restriction is narrowly tailored to serve a significant government interest, such as protecting individual privacy. The government must also demonstrate that the restriction is the least restrictive means of achieving this interest. In cases where the restriction only limits access to official records but does not prohibit the publication of information contained therein that is already in the public domain or in the possession of private persons, the state may argue that the statute strikes a balance between competing interests. This distinction is crucial in determining the constitutionality of such statutes, as it underscores the state's effort to protect privacy without unnecessarily restricting the flow of information to the public. 

Answer: (C)

evidence_store.text: The First Amendment right of access, conversely, "has been extended only to particular judicial records and documents." Stone v. Univ. of Md. Med. Sys. Corp., 855 F.2d 178, 180 (4th Cir. 1988). A court faced with a First Amendment right-of-access issue must first "determine the source of the right of access with respect to each document, because only then can it accurately weigh the competing interests at stake." Doe, 749 F.3d at 266 (citation omitted). In determining whether the First Amendment applies to a type of record or proceeding, courts employ the "experience and logic" test, asking "whether the place and process have historically been open to the press and general public" and "whether public access plays a significant positive role in the functioning of the particular process." Balt. Sun Co. v. Goetz, 886 F.2d 60, 64 (4th Cir. 1989) (citation omitted). Where the First Amendment applies, "access may be restricted only if closure is 'necessitated by a compelling government interest' and the denial of access is 'narrowly tailored to serve that interest.' " Doe, 749 F.3d at 266 (citation omitted); see Press-Enter. Co. v. Superior Court, 464 U.S. 501, 510, 104 S.Ct. 819, 78 L.Ed.2d 629 (1984) ("The presumption of openness may be overcome only by an overriding interest based on findings that closure is essential to preserve higher values .... ").

The government raises an argument, in the alternative, that even if the court were to recognize a First Amendment right of access to any of the materials requested in the Petitioner, the presumption of public access could be overcome, in part, on the basis of the compelling interest of preserving governmental and judicial resources that would be required to comply with the request to unseal the criminal investigative records filed under seal over a period of nearly 13 years. Opp. at 13-14. Petitioners object to this assertion, that administrative burden could overcome the public's First Amendment right of access to judicial records, as novel and aggressive. Reply at 4-6. This Petition certainly raises a novel issue of administrative burden because of the sheer volume of historical records that Petitioners seek to unseal for public disclosure, which is unlike the many public access cases involving the qualified First Amendment right in a particular proceeding where the presiding judge would determine whether "closure 'is necessitated by a compelling governmental interest, and is narrowly tailored to serve that interest.' " Times Mirror , 873 F.2d at 1211 n. 1 (quoting Press-Enterprise I , 464 U.S. at 509-10, 104 S.Ct. 819 ). Because the court does not recognize a First Amendment right to any of the materials requested in the Petition, it need not reach this question.

See, e.g. , United States v. Comley , 890 F.2d 539, 543-44 (1st Cir. 1989) ("Once [a prima facie showing of First Amendment infringement] is made, the burden then shifts to the government to show both a compelling need for the material sought and that there is no significantly less restrictive alternative for obtaining the information."); Wilson v. Stocker , 819 F.2d 943, 949 (10th Cir. 1987) ("The law must be substantially related to a compelling governmental interest, and must be narrowly drawn so as to be the least restrictive means of protecting that interest."); Humphreys, Hutcheson, & Moseley v. Donovan , 755 F.2d 1211, 1222 (6th Cir. 1985) (upholding the challenged provisions in part because they "are carefully tailored so that first amendment freedoms are not needlessly curtailed"); Clark v. Library of Cong. , 750 F.2d 89, 94 (D.C. Cir. 1984) ("[T]he government must demonstrate that the means chosen to further its compelling interest are those least restrictive of freedom of belief and association."); Master Printers of Am. v. Donovan , 751 F.2d 700, 705 (4th Cir. 1984) ("To survive the 'exacting scrutiny' required by the Supreme Court, ... the government must show that the disclosure and reporting requirements are justified by a compelling government interest, and that the legislation is narrowly tailored to serve that interest."); see also Perry v. Schwarzenegger , 591 F.3d 1147, 1159-61 (9th Cir. 2010) ; Dole v. Serv. Emps. Union, AFL-CIO, Local 280 , 950 F.2d 1456, 1461 (9th Cir. 1991) ; Brock v. Local 375, Plumbers Int'l Union of Am. , 860 F.2d 346, 350 (9th Cir. 1988).

As the Opinion correctly recognized, there is no general First Amendment right to access a government record. The Supreme Court has ruled that the First Amendment does not "guarantee the public a right of access to information generated or controlled by government." See Houchins v. KQED, Inc. , 438 U.S. 1, 16, 98 S.Ct. 2588, 57 L.Ed.2d 553 (1978) (Stewart, J., concurring in the judgment). And the appellate courts have complied with that principle in various contexts. See, e.g. , Ctr. for Nat'l Sec. Studies v. Dep't of Justice , 331 F.3d 918, 934 (D.C. Cir. 2003), cert. denied , 540 U.S. 1104, 124 S.Ct. 1041, 157 L.Ed.2d 888 (2004) (applying Houchins and rejecting asserted right to access government records concerning detained terrorism suspects). Although a narrow exception exists with respect to a "limited First Amendment right of access" to criminal proceedings, that exception has no bearing on Fusaro's claims. See id . (citing, inter alia, Richmond Newspapers, Inc. v. Virginia , 448 U.S. 555, 575-77, 100 S.Ct. 2814, 65 L.Ed.2d 973 (1980) ). The general rule is that of Houchins : there is no First Amendment right "to government information or sources of information within the government's control." See 438 U.S. at 15, 98 S.Ct. 2588 (plurality opinion). Rather, the decision to make government information available to the public is generally a "question of policy" for the "political branches." See id . at 12, 16, 98 S.Ct. 2588 ; see also Capital Cities Media, Inc. v. Chester , 797 F.2d 1164, 1167-71 (3d Cir. 1986) (surveying precedent and history and concluding that decision to disclose government information belongs to political branches).

In Houchins , in response to a news station's First Amendment claim for access to jails to interview inmates following reports of a prisoner's suicide, the Supreme Court plurality explained that despite the media's First Amendment right to gather information, "[t]his Court has never intimated a First Amendment guarantee of a right of access to all sources of information within government control." 438 U.S. at 9, 98 S.Ct. 2588. The Court reasoned that "[t]here is no discernible basis for a constitutional duty to disclose, or for standards governing disclosure of or access to information." Id. at 14, 98 S.Ct. 2588. Justice Stewart, concurring with the Houchins plurality, stated that "[t]he First and Fourteenth Amendments do not guarantee the public a right of access to information generated or controlled by the government, nor do they guarantee the press any basic right of access superior to that of the public generally." Id. at 16, 98 S.Ct. 2588 (Stewart, J., concurring). In a more recent case, Entler v. McKenna , the Ninth Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal of a state prisoner's claim that a state official had violated his constitutional rights by lobbying for amendments to Washington's Public Disclosure Act, stating "there is no constitutional right to public disclosure of government documents." 487 Fed. Appx. 417, 417-418 (9th Cir. 2012). These precedents provide substantial support for Defendants' argument that "laws restricting public access to records do not implicate the First Amendment at all." Dkt. 47 at 3.

label: qa_TORTS_mbe_1080
subject: TORTS
question: In a trial to a jury, Owner proved that Power Company's negligent maintenance of a transformer caused a fire that destroyed his restaurant. The jury returned a verdict for Owner in the amount of $450,000 for property loss and $500,000 for emotional distress. The trial judge entered judgment in those amounts. Power Company appealed that part of the judgment awarding $500,000 for emotional distress. On appeal, the judgment should be
final_answer:
To answer this question, we need to analyze the information provided and apply it to the scenario described. The key issue here is whether the law recognizes a claim for emotional distress incident to negligently caused property loss and under what conditions such a claim can be made.

According to the retrieved passages, the general rule is that damages for emotional distress are recoverable in cases of negligent infliction of emotional distress if the defendant's conduct creates a foreseeable risk of physical injury to the plaintiff, typically through a threat of physical impact that leads to emotional distress. However, the passages also indicate that courts have permitted recovery for emotional distress without physical symptoms in special situations where the defendant's negligence creates a great likelihood of severe emotional distress.

In the context of the question, Owner is seeking damages for emotional distress caused by the destruction of his restaurant due to Power Company's negligent maintenance of a transformer. The critical point here is whether the destruction of property (the restaurant) can give rise to a claim for emotional distress without any physical harm to the owner.

Passage 3 suggests that damages for emotional distress can be available in cases involving personal torts and medical negligence, implying that emotional distress damages are not strictly limited to cases involving physical injury. However, Passage 1 and Passage 2 imply that, in general, for a plaintiff to recover for negligent infliction of emotional distress, they must have been within the zone of danger or the defendant's conduct must have created a foreseeable risk of physical injury.

Given these considerations, the most relevant information for answering the question is found in Passage 4, which discusses the recovery of emotional distress damages under Maine law in negligence claims. It suggests that most tort actions can give rise to recovery for emotional distress, but it does not explicitly state that physical harm is required for such recovery in all negligence claims.

The correct answer, therefore, hinges on understanding that while physical harm is often a requirement for recovering emotional distress damages in negligence cases, there are exceptions and the law recognizes emotional distress as a legitimate harm in various contexts, including potentially in cases of significant property loss.

However, based on the general principles outlined in the passages, the most appropriate answer is that the judgment should be reversed unless the jury found that Owner suffered physical harm as a consequence of the emotional distress caused by his property loss. This reflects the general requirement that for emotional distress damages to be recoverable in a negligence action, there typically needs to be some physical manifestation of the distress or the plaintiff must have been within the zone of danger.

Answer: (D)

choices.A: affirmed, because Power Company negligently caused Owner's emotional distress.
choices.B: affirmed, because harm arising from emotional distress is as real as harm caused by physical impact.
choices.C: reversed, because the law does not recognize a claim for emotional distress incident to negligently caused property loss.
choices.D: reversed, unless the jury found that Owner suffered physical harm as a consequence of the emotional distress caused by his property loss.

snap_answer:
To answer this question, we need to consider the rules regarding recovery for emotional distress in the context of negligent conduct that results in property damage, as opposed to personal injury.

1. **Understanding the Context**: The scenario involves a situation where Power Company's negligence led to a fire that destroyed Owner's restaurant. The Owner was awarded damages not only for the property loss ($450,000) but also for emotional distress ($500,000).

2. **Recovery for Emotional Distress**: Generally, in tort law, a plaintiff can recover for emotional distress if it is directly related to a physical injury they suffered due to the defendant's negligence. However, when the emotional distress is solely a result of witnessing or learning about damage to property (and not a result of a direct physical harm to the plaintiff), the rules for recovery can be more restrictive.

3. **Emotional Distress without Physical Harm**: The key issue here is whether Owner can recover for emotional distress without having suffered any physical harm as a consequence of Power Company's negligence. The law varies by jurisdiction, but traditionally, courts have been hesitant to allow recovery for emotional distress that is not accompanied by physical injury, especially in cases involving only property damage.

4. **Analysis of Options**:
   - **(A)** and **(B)** suggest that the judgment should be affirmed because Power Company's negligence caused Owner's emotional distress, and emotional distress is a real harm. However, these options overlook the requirement in many jurisdictions that emotional distress must be accompanied by physical harm to be recoverable in cases of negligence involving property damage.
   - **(C)** suggests reversing the judgment because the law does not recognize a claim for emotional distress incident to negligently caused property loss. This option reflects a traditional and strict approach to recovery for emotional distress in the context of property damage.
   - **(D)** suggests reversing the judgment unless the jury found that Owner suffered physical harm as a consequence of the emotional distress. This option acknowledges that recovery for emotional distress might be allowed if it resulted in physical harm, which aligns with the principle that emotional distress is more likely to be compensable if it leads to physical consequences.

5. **Conclusion**: Given the traditional reluctance of courts to allow recovery for emotional distress not resulting in physical harm, especially in the context of property damage, the most appropriate answer reflects the requirement for physical harm to justify recovery for emotional distress in such cases.

Answer: (D)

According to the principles of tort law, recovery for emotional distress is generally allowed when it is directly related to a physical injury suffered due to the defendant's negligence. However, in cases involving only property damage, courts have traditionally been hesitant to allow recovery for emotional distress without accompanying physical harm. The rule that emotional distress must be accompanied by physical harm to be recoverable in cases of negligence involving property damage is a longstanding principle, but an exception may apply if the emotional distress results in physical harm, thereby potentially justifying recovery.

Answer: (D)

evidence_store.text: If a person is not within the zone of danger, they are precluded from recovery for negligent infliction of emotional distress. A defendant breaches a duty to avoid negligent infliction of emotional distress when he creates a foreseeable risk of physical injury to the plaintiff through causing a threat of physical impact that leads to emotional distress. Damages generally are recoverable only if the defendant's conduct causes some physical injury, rather than purely emotional distress (although a severe shock to the nervous system that causes physical symptoms is sufficient). If plaintiff's distress is caused by threat of physical impact to her, she must have been within the zone of danger.

In the usual case, the duty to avoid negligent infliction of emotional distress is breached when defendant creates a foreseeable risk of physical injury to plaintiff, typically by causing a threat of physical impact that leads to emotional distress, and plaintiff can recover for physical injury caused solely by the distress. In special situations, however, courts have permitted plaintiff to recover in the absence of physical symptoms where defendant's negligence creates a great likelihood of severe emotional distress.

¶ 44 Finally, Pfeifer's and NorthShore's reliance on Robbins in support of the argument that Ryan's alleged injuries are not severe enough to satisfy "the physical illness or injury requirement of an emotional distress claim" is inapposite, because Robbins involved a freestanding negligent infliction of emotional distress claim rather than a claim seeking damages for emotional distress. Furthermore, damages for emotional distress are available to prevailing plaintiffs in cases involving personal torts, such as defamation, conversion, and misappropriation of identity ( Clark , 2011 IL 108656, ¶ 111, 353 Ill.Dec. 254, 955 N.E.2d 1065 ), and medical negligence ( Babikian v. Mruz , 2011 IL (App) 1st 102579, ¶ 19, 353 Ill.Dec. 831, 956 N.E.2d 959 ). We see no principled basis for why a person aggrieved under the Act should not be permitted to recover emotional distress damages arising out of violations of section 10(d) of the Act.

Zurich does make the sweeping contention in its reply brief that emotional distress damages under Maine law may be recovered in negligence claims only for conduct that resulted in physical injuries, presumably to support the contention that damages may not be recovered in such claims for physical injuries that result from emotional distress. But, aside from the fact that arguments that are made for the first time in reply briefs are waived, see United States v. Torres, 162 F.3d 6, 11 (1st Cir. 1998), Zurich does not cite to any precedent that supports its broad assertion about the limited circumstances in which damages for emotional distress may be recovered for the tort of negligence in Maine. Nor does Zurich address the fact that the Law Court stated in Curtis that "most tort actions" can give rise to recovery for emotional distress. Curtis, 784 A.2d at 26. Thus, we treat as waived for lack of development any argument that a negligence claim for an omission such as was alleged to have occurred here cannot give rise to emotional distress damages under Maine law. See United States v. Zannino, 895 F.2d 1, 17 (1st Cir. 1990).

Extreme and outrageous conduct is an element of the prima facie case for intentional infliction of emotional distress. Mere intrusion on the plaintiff's property does not constitute intentional infliction of emotional distress. Physical injury is not required to recover for this tort.

To answer this question, let's break down the key concepts related to facial and as-applied challenges as provided in the retrieved passages.

1. **Facial Challenge**: This involves challenging the constitutionality of a statute based solely on its text, without considering how it applies to a specific individual or circumstance. Essentially, the argument is that the statute is unconstitutional on its face, meaning it is inherently flawed regardless of how it is applied.

2. **As-Applied Challenge**: This type of challenge argues that a statute, while potentially constitutional on its face (i.e., the text of the statute itself does not violate the Constitution), becomes unconstitutional when applied to a particular person under specific circumstances. The focus here is on the application of the statute rather than the statute's text itself.

Given these definitions, let's analyze the options provided in the context of the "Capitol steps" statute:

- **(A) Constitutional both on its face and as applied to Doe**: This option suggests that the statute does not have any inherent constitutional flaws (facial challenge) and also does not violate Doe's constitutional rights when applied (as-applied challenge). Without specific details about the statute's content or its application to Doe, it's difficult to assess the validity of this claim directly from the provided passages.

- **(B) Constitutional on its face but unconstitutional as applied to Doe**: This option implies that while the statute itself does not contain inherent constitutional violations, its application to Doe's specific circumstances results in a violation of Doe's constitutional rights. This aligns with the definition of an as-applied challenge.

- **(C) Unconstitutional on its face, because it applies to all working days**: This option suggests a facial challenge, arguing that the statute's application to all working days makes it inherently unconstitutional. However, the mere fact that a statute applies broadly does not necessarily render it unconstitutional on its face.

- **(D) Unconstitutional on its face, because it concerns the State Capitol**: Similar to option (C), this suggests a facial challenge based on the statute's subject matter (the State Capitol). The location or subject matter of a statute, by itself, does not typically determine its constitutionality.

Without specific details about the "Capitol steps" statute, including its content and how it applies to Doe, it's challenging to definitively choose between these options based solely on the provided passages. However, the question seems to prompt for an analysis based on typical legal reasoning regarding facial and as-applied challenges.

Given the information provided and typical legal reasoning, **(B)** seems like a plausible answer because it acknowledges the distinction between a statute's facial constitutionality and its application to a specific individual. This option recognizes that a statute can be generally constitutional but still violate an individual's rights under specific circumstances, which aligns with the principles of as-applied challenges outlined in the retrieved passages.

Williams' testimony about the driver's statement should be
final_answer:
To determine the admissibility of the driver's statement as testified by Williams, we need to analyze it under the rules of evidence, specifically focusing on the hearsay rule and its exceptions.

1. **Hearsay Rule**: The hearsay rule generally excludes out-of-court statements offered to prove the truth of the matter asserted. The driver's statement to Williams is an out-of-court statement, so it initially appears to be hearsay.

2. **Exceptions to the Hearsay Rule**: There are several exceptions to the hearsay rule, including the present sense impression and excited utterance exceptions. 

   - **Present Sense Impression**: This exception applies to statements describing or explaining an event or condition made while the declarant was perceiving the event or condition, or immediately thereafter. For a statement to qualify as a present sense impression, it must:
     1. Be made simultaneously, or almost simultaneously, with the occurrence of an event or act.
     2. Describe or explain the event.
     3. Be made by a declarant who perceived the event with their own senses.
   
   - **Excited Utterance**: This exception applies to statements made while the declarant was under the stress of excitement caused by an event or condition. However, the details provided do not specifically indicate that the driver's statement was made under the stress of excitement.

Given the information:
- The driver's statement was made a few minutes after the accident, which suggests it was not made simultaneously or immediately after the event.
- The statement describes the accident and the driver's subsequent actions (following the blue convertible to the drive-in restaurant).

Considering these points, the driver's statement does not perfectly fit the criteria for a present sense impression because it was not made simultaneously or immediately after the event. However, it's crucial to note that the statement's admissibility might still be argued under other exceptions or considerations, but based strictly on the present sense impression criteria as outlined, it does not fit.

Therefore, without a clear indication that the statement falls under another exception (like excited utterance, which is not clearly applicable from the given details), and given that it does not meet the criteria for a present sense impression, the statement would be considered hearsay not within any exception provided in the question context.

**Answer: (C)**

hearsay: Hearsay is an out-of-court statement offered to prove the truth of whatever it asserts, which is then offered in evidence to prove the truth of the matter. The problem with hearsay is that when the person being quoted is not present, it becomes impossible to establish credibility. As a result, hearsay evidence is generally not admissible in court. However, there are exceptions to the hearsay rule, which are explained below. The first exception to the hearsay rule is the "excited utterance" exception. This exception applies when someone makes a statement during a startling event, in the heat of the moment, potentially providing an unguarded and accurate piece of information. This exception is most applicable in criminal cases, as the rationale behind it is that during or immediately following a criminal act, a person is not likely to have the presence of mind to lie or give false statements. In order for a statement to qualify as an excited utterance, it must have been made in conjunction with an event that would be so overwhelming as to discount the possibility of fabrication. The second exception to the hearsay rule is the "statements against interest" exception. This exception applies to statements or actions that adversely affect the party who made the statement. These statements do not need to be formal admissions. The theory behind this exception is that a person would not fabricate a statement that is adverse to their own best interest. However, the witness offering the hearsay testimony may not be telling the truth, but that goes to credibility, not admissibility. See: Federal Rules of Evidence 804(b)(3) The third exception to the hearsay rule is the "matter of record" exception. This exception allows for official government records, private business records, prior court decisions or documents, and prior testimony of an unavailable witness to be admitted as evidence. In order for these records and documents to be admissible, they must be verifiable in some way, such as with a notarized original or a witness who can attest to their authenticity. For federal trials, the rules for hearsay are contained within Article VIII of the Federal Rules of Evidence, Rules 801-807.  Rule 801(c) of the FRE defines hearsay.  Rule 802 prohibits the admissibility of hearsay.  Rule 803, Rule 804 and Rule 807 list exceptions to the rule against hearsay.  Rule 805 discusses hearsay within hearsay.   Rule 806 discusses the credibility of the declarant of the hearsay.   See: Federal Rules of Evidence › ARTICLE VIII. HEARSAY › Rule 803. Exceptions to the Rule Against Hearsay  Although hearsay rules differ by jurisdiction, many state hearsay rules are modeled after the Federal Rules of Evidence.  It is important to remember that even with these exceptions, the judge has the discretion to determine whether or not to admit hearsay evidence. [Last updated in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team]

For a hearsay statement to be admissible pursuant to the present sense impression exception, the statement must be made simultaneously, or almost simultaneously, with the occurrence of an event or act, the statement must describe or explain the event; and the declarant must perceive the event with his own senses. State v. Smith , 265 S.W.3d 874, 879 (Mo. App. 2008) (citing 2 McCormick on Evidence § 271, at 251 (6th ed. 2006) ). These statements have certain indicia of trustworthiness to support their admissibility. Id. Errors in memory and time for calculated misstatements are not present because the statements are made as declarant perceives the event or immediately thereafter. Id. Further, in most cases, "a witness will have observed the event and can corroborate the hearsay statement, and the declarant will often be available at trial for cross-examination to verify his or her credibility." Id.

question: If Innes asserts a claim against Glass, the most likely result is that she will
final_answer:
To determine the most likely result of Innes asserting a claim against Glass, we need to analyze the situation based on the principles of negligence as outlined in the provided passages. The key elements to establish a negligence claim are: (1) the defendant owed a duty of care to the plaintiff, (2) the defendant breached that duty, (3) the breach was the actual and proximate cause of the plaintiff's injury, and (4) the plaintiff suffered damages.

In this scenario, Innes would need to prove that Glass owed her a duty of care, that Glass breached this duty, and that the breach directly caused her injury. The duty of care is typically established if the defendant could have reasonably foreseen that their actions could cause harm to the plaintiff.

Option (A) suggests that a user of a product is held to the same standard as the manufacturer. While both manufacturers and users have duties of care, the nature and scope of these duties can differ significantly. Manufacturers have a duty to ensure their products are safe for use and provide adequate warnings, whereas users have a duty to use products in a reasonably safe manner. This option does not directly address the foreseeability of harm or the breach of duty by Glass.

Option (B) implies that because the employees of Glass caused the fumes to enter Innes' area, they are liable. However, for Innes to recover, she must show that Glass had a duty to prevent the fumes from entering her area and that they breached this duty. Simply causing the fumes does not automatically imply a breach of duty without considering whether Glass could have reasonably foreseen the harm.

Option (C) states that Glass used the glue for its intended purposes. If Glass used the glue as intended and had no reason to believe that doing so would cause harm to Innes, this could suggest that Glass did not breach its duty of care. However, this option does not consider whether the use of the glue in its intended manner could have been done in a way that minimized risks to others, such as providing adequate ventilation.

Option (D) suggests that Innes will not recover because the employees of Glass had no reason to know that the fumes could injure Innes. This aligns with the principle that no duty is imposed upon a person to take precautions against events that cannot reasonably be foreseen (as stated in Source 2). If Glass could not have reasonably foreseen that the fumes from the glue would cause injury to Innes, then they did not owe her a duty of care in this specific context, making it unlikely for Innes to recover.

Based on the principles outlined, the most critical factor is whether Glass could have reasonably foreseen the risk of injury to Innes from the fumes. If they could not, then they did not owe her a duty of care regarding this specific risk.

Answer: (D)

Negligence requires demonstrating a duty of care, a breach of that duty, causation, and damages. If Glass's employees failed to take reasonable care to prevent the fumes from affecting others (such as using the glue in a well-ventilated area or warning others), this could support Innes's claim.

4. **Analyzing Option (C)**: This option suggests that using a product for its intended purposes automatically absolves the user of liability. However, this is not necessarily true. Even if a product is used as intended, if the user fails to follow safety instructions or takes no precautions to prevent harm to others, they can still be held liable. The key factor is not just the intended use but how the product was used and whether reasonable care was taken to avoid harming others.

5. **Analyzing Option (D)**: This option implies that if Glass's employees had no reason to know that the fumes could injure Innes, they cannot be held liable. However, in many jurisdictions, the standard for negligence includes the concept of "should have known" rather than "did know." If it was reasonable for Glass's employees to anticipate that the fumes could cause harm to others in the building, their lack of actual knowledge might not protect them from liability.

Considering these analyses, the most compelling argument for Innes recovering would be based on the actions of Glass's employees directly causing the harm (the fumes entering her area) due to potential negligence in their use of the glue.
