evidence_store.text: To distinguish between vested remainders and contingent remainders, a court must determine whether at the time the instrument takes effect there is “a person who in his own right, or as a part of his estate, would take all of this property if [the life estate] ended now.” If there is such a person, then the remainder is vested subject to partial or complete defeasance. If no such person is identifiable, then the remainder is subject to a condition precedent and is a contingent remainder. In determining the type of remainder created, Georgia has consistently followed two common law principles: (a) the law favors construing “conditions to be subsequent” and (b) the law favors the “vesting of remainders in all cases of doubt,” which is also called the “early vesting” of remainders. Professor Chaffin has noted that “[a]lthough it is possible to create nondescendible defeasible remainders by using very clear conditions of survivorship by way of condition subsequent, it is difficult to do so in view of the preference for defeasibly vested rather than contingent interests.”

Under the Rule Against Perpetuities, an interest in property is not valid unless it will vest, if at all, not later than 21 years after a life in being at the creation of the interest. The validity of interests under the Rule is determined at the time the interests are created, taking into account the facts then existing. The "lives in being plus 21 years" period begins to run, and the measuring lives used to show the validity of an interest must be in existence, at that time. An interest is classified as a contingent remainder if the condition precedent of their reaching a certain age before taking an interest. An est would not have been vested if their taking was subject to a condition precedent. As a rule of thumb, remainders always follow life estates. An executory interest is an interest that divests the interest of another.

The rule against perpetuities in Virginia voids a contingent remainder or executory interest, created inter vivos or by will, which may, by some possibility, however unlikely that possibility may be, vest beyond a life or lives in being at the effective date of the instrument creating the interest, plus 21 years and 10 months. See Pleasants v. Pleasants, 6 Va. (2 Call) 319 (1798); Rose v. Rose, 191 Va. 171, 60 S.E.2d 45 (1950);  Burruss v. Baldwin, 199 Va. 883, 103 S.E.2d 249 (1958); White v. National Bank, 212 Va. 568, 186 S.E.2d 21 (1972).

A life tenant's relation to the remainderman is a quasi-fiduciary one in the sense that he must exercise reasonable care to preserve the property intact for transmission to the remainderman and in that he can legally do nothing to prejudice or defeat the estate of the remainderman. 1 Tiffany, Real Property § 68 (3rd ed. 1939); 31 C.J.S. Estates § 34 (1964); 51 Am.Jur.2d, Life Tenants and Remaindermen §§ 27, 28 (1970). "[N]o such fiduciary relations exist between a life tenant and his remainderman as to make applicable to their transactions the rules of equity which govern trustees and cestuis que trustent, and preclude the life tenant from acquiring by gift or purchase from the remainderman his estate in remainder." Muzzy v. Muzzy, 364 Mo. 373, 380, 261 S.W.2d 927, 931 (1953). The life tenant has the obligation to list and pay the taxes on the property. G.S. 105-302(c)(8); G.S. 105-384. See Smith v. Smith, 261 N.C. 278, 134 S.E.2d 331 (1964); Meadows v. Meadows, 216 N.C. 413, 5 S.E.2d 128 (1939). Therefore he cannot defeat the estate of the remainderman by allowing the land to be sold for taxes and taking title in himself by purchase at the tax sale. See Farabow v. Perry, 223 N.C. 21, 26, 25 S.E.2d 173, 176 (1943); Creech v. Wilder, 212 N.C. 162, 166, 193 S.E. 281, 284 (1937); Miller v. Marriner, 187 N.C. 449, 457, 121 S.E. 770, 774 (1924); 51 Am.Jur.2d, supra, § 255; 1 Tiffany, supra, § 68. The life tenant's purchase at a tax sale "is regarded as a payment of the tax, and the owner of the future interest is regarded as still holding under his original title." Simes and Smith, The Law of Future Interests, § 1700 (2d ed. 1956). In addition to the taxes, absent a different stipulation in the instrument creating the life estate, a life tenant owes a duty to the remaindermen to pay the interest accruing during the period of his estate on a mortgage encumbrance given prior to the creation of the life estate and remainder or reversion, at least to the extent of the income or rental value of the property. Simes and Smith, supra, § 1697. See 31 C.J.S. Estates § 48 (1964); 51 Am.Jur.2d, supra, § 277. "Being bound to pay the taxes and interest, he [a life tenant] cannot acquire a tax title or good title based on his failing to pay taxes or interest. He is a trustee to this extent." Miller v. Marriner, supra at 457, 121 S.E. at 774. If an encumbrance is foreclosed because of the default on the part of the life tenant in the payment of interest or otherwise, and he becomes the purchaser at the foreclosure sale, "he thereby restores the life estate and the estate in the remainder." 1 Tiffany, supra § 68. See Morehead v. Harris, 262 N.C. 330, 338, 137 S.E.2d 174, 182 (1964); Restatement of Property §§ 129, 130, 131, 149 (1936); 31 C.J.S. Estates § 35 (1964). In respect to a prior mortgage lien on the whole estate, unless obligated by the instrument creating his estate, the life tenant's only duty to the remainderman is to pay the interest. He is under no obligation to pay any part of the principal. 31 C.J.S. Estates § 48 (1964); 51 Am.Jur.2d, supra, § 275. When a life tenant, in order to preserve his estate, "pays off an encumbrance upon the fee or estate property, whether the encumbrance is a mortgage, lien, charge or other type of encumbrance, he is entitled to reimbursement from the owners of future interests, such as reversioners or remaindermen, to the extent of their interest in the property which was subject to the encumbrance." 51 Am. Jur.2d, supra, § 275. See 31 C.J.S. Estates § 48 (1964); 1 Tiffany, supra, § 63. He "has a lien on the future interest for the amount which its owner is under a duty to pay." Simes and Smith, supra, § 1697. See Farabow v. Perry, supra, 223 N.C. at 26, 25 S.E.2d at 176; Creech v. Wilder, supra, 212 N.C. at 166, 193 S.E. at 284. When a mortgage falls due during the period of the life estate the question arises: Who has the burden of paying the principal? In Simes and Smith, The Law of Future Interests, § 1697 (2d ed. 1956), the question is answered as follows: "Courts have generally indicated that life tenant and reversioner or remainderman must each pay his due proportion of this amount. To require them to share the burden would seem to be just. By paying off the mortgage, the value of both life estate and future interest have been increased in proportion to their respective values. Hence it would seem that the due proportion would be based upon the respective values of life estate and the remainder or reversion.. . . The cases, however, are not clear as to what a due proportion of the principal is." The foregoing statement accords with the Restatement of Property § 132 (1936). Explanatory Comment e. under this section says that the proportionate contributions of the life estate and future interests to the payment of an encumbrance "are computable by employing the mortality tables and rate of interest regularly employed in valuing an estate for life in the state wherein the affected land is located." Id. at p. 433. See Faulkenburg v. Windorf, 194 Minn. 154, 157, 259 N.W. 802, 804 (1935). In Comment a. to § 132, supra, it is noted that absent some special provision in the instrument creating the estate for life, the life tenant has no duty to contribute from his other assets to the payment of the encumbrance. "He can, however, be compelled to choose between giving up his estate for life and making a contribution, from his other assets, to the new investment of capital." Id. at p. 431. When a mortgage, deed of trust or other encumbrance on the whole estate, the burden of which does not fall solely on the life tenant, is foreclosed, the life tenant may purchase the property at the sale in order to protect his interest. Yet he cannot purchase the fee on a foreclosure sale so as to exclude the remainderman if the remainderman is willing to contribute his share of the cost of acquisition within a reasonable time. "It is uniformly held that the purchase of land by a life tenant at a foreclosure sale under a mortgage or deed of trust will be deemed to have been made for the benefit of the remainderman or reversioner *745 if he contributes his portion of the purchase money within a reasonable time." 51 Am. Jur.2d supra, § 280. See Witcher v. Hanley, 299 Mo. 696, 253 S.W. 1002 (1923); Hager v. Connolly, 204 Ky. 147, 263 S.W. 723 (1924); Ward v. Chambless, 238 Ala. 165, 189 So. 890 (1939); Drane v. Smith, 271 Ala. 54, 122 So. 2d 135 (1960); 51 Am.Jur.2d, supra, § 279; 1 Tiffany, supra, § 68.

Francis and William argue a child who predeceased Mary could not have exercised the testamentary POA in § 3.1 because before Mary's death each child had only a "contingent remainder interest" in the Trust and therefore would have had "no interest to appoint at the time of the child's death." However, Francis and William cite no legal authority in support of the proposition that a child who predeceased Mary could not have conveyed his or her contingent remainder interest in the Trust via his or her will. We need not address arguments that are unsupported by references to legal authority. See State v. Pettit , 171 Wis. 2d 627, 646, 492 N.W.2d 633 (Ct. App. 1992).

question: If Innes asserts a claim against Paint Company, the most likely result is that she will
final_answer:
To determine the most likely result if Innes asserts a claim against Paint Company, we need to analyze the elements required to establish a claim for negligence or products liability, as these are the most relevant areas of law given the context of the question.

1. **Negligence Claim**: For Innes to recover against Paint Company based on negligence, she must prove that Paint Company owed her a duty of care, breached that duty, and that the breach was the proximate cause of her injury. The sources (Source 2, Source 3, Source 4, Source 5) all outline similar elements for a negligence claim, emphasizing the importance of duty, breach, causation, and damages.

2. **Products Liability Claim**: If the claim is based on strict liability in tort for products liability, Innes would need to prove that Paint Company is a commercial supplier, that the product (in this case, the glue) was defective and unreasonably dangerous, that the defect caused her injury, and that she suffered damages. Source 1 provides the elements for a products liability claim based on strict liability.

Given these requirements, let's evaluate the options:

- **(A) recover if she can recover against Steel**: This option implies a direct relationship between recovery against Steel and recovery against Paint Company, which isn't necessarily true based on the principles of negligence or products liability. Each defendant's liability is determined by their own actions or omissions.

- **(B) recover, because Innes was an invitee of a tenant in the building**: This option introduces the concept of premises liability, which isn't directly relevant to a claim against a manufacturer like Paint Company unless the building owner or another party is involved.

- **(C) not recover unless Paint Company was negligent**: This option aligns with the principles of negligence as outlined in the sources. For Innes to recover against Paint Company, she would indeed need to prove that Paint Company was negligent, assuming the claim is based on negligence. If the claim is based on strict liability, the focus would be on the product being defective and unreasonably dangerous, but negligence on the part of Paint Company could still be a factor in certain aspects of the claim.

- **(D) not recover, because the glue came in a sealed package**: This option doesn't directly address the legal principles necessary for determining liability. The condition of the packaging (sealed or not) might be relevant to certain aspects of the claim (e.g., whether Paint Company had a duty to warn about the contents), but it's not a definitive factor in determining recovery.

Considering the information provided and the principles of negligence and products liability, the most accurate statement regarding the likely outcome of Innes' claim against Paint Company is related to the necessity of proving negligence or defect, depending on the nature of the claim.

Answer: (C)

choices.A: recover if she can recover against Steel
choices.B: recover, because Innes was an invitee of a tenant in the building
choices.C: not recover unless Paint Company was negligent
choices.D: not recover, because the glue came in a sealed package

evidence_store.text: To establish a prima facie case in products liability based on strict liability in tort, the following elements must be proved: (i) strict duty owed by a commercial supplier; (ii) production or sale of a defective product; (iii) actual and proximate cause; and (iv) damages. To establish the second element, the plaintiff need prove only that the product in fact is so defective as to be "unreasonably dangerous." In failure to warn cases, the plaintiff is entitled to a presumption that an adequate warning would have been read and heeded, although the jury could very well reject that presumption. Nevertheless, whether inadequate warnings made the product so defective as to be unreasonably dangerous is a question of fact for the jury.

To state a claim for negligence against defendant, as plaintiff asserts in her complaint, plaintiff must allege facts establishing that defendant "failed to exercise proper care in the performance of some legal duty owed [to plaintiff] and that the breach of this duty was the proximate cause of [plaintiff's] injury." Goodman v. Wenco Foods, Inc., 333 N.C. 1, 18, 423 S.E.2d 444 (1992).

Appellants assert Rahall's liability is grounded in negligence. To establish negligence, a plaintiff must show: (1) the defendant owed a duty of care to the plaintiff; (2) the defendant breached the duty by a negligent act or omission; (3) the defendant's breach was the actual and proximate cause of the plaintiff's injury; and (4) the plaintiff suffered an injury or damages. Steinke v. SC Dep't of Labor, Licensing and Regulation , 336 S.C. 373, 387, 520 S.E.2d 142, 149 (1999). "An essential element in a cause of action for negligence is the existence of a legal duty owed by the defendant to the plaintiff." Huggins v. Citibank, NA. , 355 S.C. 329, 332, 585 S.E.2d 275, 276 (2003). In the absence of a duty, there can be no negligence. Id. at 332, 585 S.E.2d at 277.

Noel's claim is one of negligence. In order to prevail on a claim of negligence, the plaintiff must prove that the defendant owed a duty to the plaintiff, that the defendant breached that duty, and that the breach was the proximate cause of the plaintiff's damages. Branscumb v. Freeman , 360 Ark. 171, 200 S.W.3d 411 (2004).

To state a claim for negligence under Massachusetts law, a plaintiff must demonstrate that 1) "the defendant owed the plaintiff a duty of reasonable care", 2) "the defendant breached this duty", 3) "damage resulted" and 4) "there was a causal relation between the breach of the duty and the damage". Jupin v. Kask, 447 Mass. 141, 849 N.E.2d 829, 834-35 (2006).

label: qa_nan_mbe_384

question: Harris, a heroin addict, broke into a home and took several cameras and watches, which he promptly pawned to obtain cash with which to purchase a "fix." Harris was later charged with larceny of the cameras and watches.
final_answer:
To determine the correct answer, let's analyze the scenario and the provided options in the context of larceny.

Larceny is defined as the taking and carrying away of the tangible personal property of another by trespass, with the intent to permanently deprive the person of his interest in the property. The key elements include:
1. Unlawful taking
2. Carrying away (asportation)
3. Of personal property of another
4. With the intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property

Given Harris's actions:
- He broke into a home, which satisfies the trespass element.
- He took several cameras and watches, which are tangible personal properties of another.
- He carried them away (asportation) by promptly pawning them to obtain cash, indicating an intent to permanently deprive the owner of these items.

Now, let's examine the provided options:
(A) Commonwealth v. Mason - This case involves two sisters taking a dog with the intent to conceal it until a reward is offered. This scenario does involve an initial trespass and an intent to deprive the owner of the property (the dog), which aligns with larceny elements.

(B) Saferite v. State - This case involves two men taking a car with the intention of visiting one of their wives, not with the intent to permanently deprive the owner of the car. This does not align well with Harris's scenario, as the intent in Saferite v. State was not to steal.

(C) People v. Noblett - This case involves a tenant who accepts rent from a sublessee but fails to provide possession of the apartment. While this involves deception and failure to fulfill an agreement, it doesn't directly relate to the physical taking and carrying away of personal property with the intent to permanently deprive the owner, as seen in Harris's case.

(D) King v. Pear - This case involves a defendant hiring a horse with the intent to sell it, which he does. This scenario involves an initial lawful possession (hiring the horse) that turns into an unlawful act (selling the horse), which aligns with the concept of larceny, especially considering the intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property.

Considering Harris's actions and the definitions of larceny provided in the sources, Harris's case most closely aligns with scenarios where there is a clear intent to permanently deprive the owner of personal property through trespass and asportation. Among the given options, (A) Commonwealth v. Mason, is the most relevant because it directly involves the taking of property (a dog) with the intent to conceal it until a reward is offered, which implies an intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property, similar to Harris's intent when taking the cameras and watches.

Answer: (A)

choices.A: Commonwealth v. Mason. Two sisters see a wealthy neighbor's pedigreed dog on the street. They take the dog home, intending to conceal it until the owner offers a reward. Held, guilty of larceny.
choices.B: Saferite v. State. Two young men saw a motor car on the street with the keys in the ignition lock. They drove the car to a neighboring town with the intention, they said, of visiting the wife of one of them. The car was wrecked on their way back. Conviction for larceny reversed.
choices.C: People v. Noblett. Defendant, a tenant of a city apartment, advertised it for sublease. Will agreed to sublease for three months, and on March 12 paid Defendant $550, the total agreed rental. Will was to receive possession on March 20, but possession was never given him. Held, not guilty of common law larceny.
choices.D: King v. Pear. From a stablekeeper, Defendant hired a horse to go to Sutton and back, saying he would be back at 8 p.m. He did not return. Invest. gation showed that Defendant had given a false address, and that he had sold the horse the same day. Conviction of larceny affirmed.

snap_answer:
To determine whether Harris is guilty of larceny, we must analyze the elements of larceny and apply them to the given scenario. Larceny is the trespassory taking and carrying away of the personal property of another with the intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property.

In Harris's case, he broke into a home and took several cameras and watches, which he then pawned to obtain cash to buy heroin. The key issue here is whether Harris had the intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property. 

Looking at the provided examples:
- (A) Commonwealth v. Mason: The sisters took the dog with the intent to conceal it until a reward was offered, implying they did not intend to permanently deprive the owner of the dog. However, they were still found guilty of larceny, suggesting that the intent to permanently deprive can be inferred from the actions and circumstances.
- (B) Saferite v. State: The young men took the car with the intention of visiting one of their wives and then returning, which implies a lack of intent to permanently deprive the owner of the car. Their conviction for larceny was reversed, indicating that their stated intent was believed, and thus they did not commit larceny.
- (C) People v. Noblett: The defendant took money for a sublease but never provided possession of the apartment. This case involves a failure to provide a service rather than the taking of physical property, so it's less directly relevant to Harris's situation.
- (D) King v. Pear: The defendant hired a horse with the intent to sell it, demonstrating an intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property. His conviction for larceny was affirmed.

Given these examples, Harris's actions—breaking into a home, taking property, and then pawning it for cash—demonstrate a clear intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property. The fact that he did so to satisfy his addiction does not negate the intent to deprive, which is a necessary element for larceny.

Therefore, based on the analysis of the intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property, Harris is guilty of larceny.

Answer: (A)

The intent to permanently deprive can be inferred from the circumstances and actions of the defendant, and the fact that the taking was for personal gain or to satisfy an addiction does not negate this intent. As established in cases such as Commonwealth v. Mason, where the defendants' actions implied an intent to conceal the property until a reward was offered, and King v. Pear, where the defendant's intent to sell the horse demonstrated an intent to permanently deprive the owner, Harris's actions in breaking into a home, taking property, and pawning it for cash demonstrate a clear intent to permanently deprive the owner of the property, thereby satisfying the elements of larceny.

evidence_store.text: Larceny is the taking and carrying away of the tangible personal property of another by trespass, with the intent to permanently deprive the person of his interest in the property. Under the continuing trespass doctrine, if a defendant takes property with a wrongful state of mind but without the intent to steal, and later, while still in possession of it, forms the intent to steal it, the trespass involved in the initial wrongful taking is regarded as "continuing" and the defendant is guilty of larceny.

Although the exact state statutes may vary, larceny typically involves (i) the unlawful taking (ii) of the property of another (iii) without their consent (iv) and with the specific intent to permanently deprive the owner of that property. However, under the continuing trespass doctrine, if a defendant takes property with a wrongful state of mind, but without the intent to commit larceny, and later, while still in possession of the property, forms the intent to steal it, the trespass involved in the initial wrongful taking is regarded as "continuing," and the defendant is guilty of larceny. Burglary is the breaking and entering into the dwelling of another at nighttime with the intent to commit a felony therein. The intent to commit a felony must exist at the time of the breaking and entering.

General Laws c. 266, § 30 (1), provides that "[w]hoever steals, ... or whoever unlawfully, and with intent to steal or embezzle, converts, or secretes with intent to convert, the property of another ... shall be guilty of larceny." To support a conviction of larceny by stealing, the Commonwealth must prove "(1) the unlawful taking and (2) carrying away (asportation) (3) of personal property of another (4) with the specific intent to deprive the person of the property permanently." Commonwealth v. Vickers, 60 Mass. App. Ct. 24, 27 (2003). See Commonwealth v. Mills, 436 Mass. 387, 394 (2002). "[I]t is well-recognized in Massachusetts and elsewhere that taking goods beyond a store's premises is not a necessary precondition to a larceny conviction; the element of asportation may be satisfied if the defendant's behavior establishes that he or she removed the goods from the store's control to his or her own." Vickers, supra. See Commonwealth v. Davis, 41 Mass. App. Ct. 901, 901 (1996). Such a showing is made where "there is evidence, typically proof of concealment, that is inconsistent with an intent to complete a purchase." Vickers, supra at 28 (while shopping, defendant put clothes inside her beach bag, dropped them after realizing she was being watched by security, and, when confronted, said she did not have anything in bag "anymore").

The crime of larceny requires the “ ‘ “taking by trespass and carrying away by any person of the goods or personal property of another, without the latter's consent and with the felonious intent permanently to deprive the owner of his property and to convert it to the taker's own use.” ’ ” State v. Boykin, 78 N.C.App. 572, 576, 337 S.E.2d 678, 681 (1985) (citations omitted).

Larceny is the taking and asportation of the personal property of another by trespass and with the intent to permanently deprive the person of his interest in the property. Elements common to embezzlement are as follows: (1) the property must belong to a person other than the accused, such as an employer or principal; (2) the property must be converted subsequent to the defendant's original and lawful possession of it; (3) the defendant must be in a position of trust, so that the property is held by him or her pursuant to some fiduciary duty; and (4) the defendant must have an intent to defraud the owner at the time of the conversion. If the defendant did not have especially broad power over the property and it was not given to the defendant by a third party, the defendant merely had custody, not possession, of the property.

label: qa_TORTS_mbe_943
subject: TORTS
question: Assume for this question only that the state has retained the common-law rule pertaining to contribution and that the state's comparative negligence statute provides for a system of pure comparative negligence but abolishes joint and several liability. If Pat chooses to execute against Donald alone, she will be entitled to collect at most
final_answer:
To solve this, let's break down the information provided and apply it step by step to the scenario.

1. **Pure Comparative Negligence**: This means that the plaintiff (Pat) can recover some percentage of her damages regardless of her level of negligence, as long as she is not 100% at fault. Her recovery will be reduced by her proportionate share of the fault.

2. **Abolition of Joint and Several Liability**: This means that each defendant is only liable for their proportionate share of the damages based on their percentage of fault. Pat cannot collect the entire amount from one defendant; she can only collect that defendant's proportionate share.

3. **Common-Law Rule Pertaining to Contribution Retained**: Normally, under joint and several liability, if one defendant pays more than their share, they can seek contribution from the other defendants for the excess. However, since joint and several liability is abolished in this scenario, the common-law rule on contribution might not directly apply as it typically would. Instead, we focus on the proportionate liability based on fault.

Given that we don't have specific percentages of fault for Pat, Donald, and Drew, let's assume hypothetical percentages to understand the concept:
- Pat's fault: 40%
- Donald's fault: 30%
- Drew's fault: 30%

If the total damages are $100,000, then:
- Pat can recover 60% of $100,000 (since she is 40% at fault), which is $60,000.
- This $60,000 would be split between Donald and Drew based on their fault. So, Donald would be liable for 30% of the $100,000 (his share of the fault), which is $30,000, and similarly, Drew would be liable for $30,000.

If Pat chooses to execute against Donald alone, she can only collect Donald's share, which is $30,000 based on his proportionate fault. Since joint and several liability is abolished, Pat cannot collect more from Donald than his proportionate share.

Now, considering the options provided and applying our understanding:
- **(A)** Incorrect because it implies joint and several liability, which is abolished.
- **(B)** Incorrect because it suggests Donald can collect from Drew, which might not directly apply under the abolished joint and several liability and retained common-law contribution rule in this specific comparative negligence context.
- **(C)** Correct because Pat can collect $30,000 from Donald (his proportionate share), and since Donald cannot be held for more than his share due to the abolition of joint and several liability, he cannot collect from Drew.
- **(D)** Incorrect because it suggests Pat cannot recover anything due to her percentage of fault, which is not accurate under pure comparative negligence.

Answer: (C)

choices.B: $30,000 from Donald, and then Donald will be entitled to collect $10,000 from Drew.
choices.C: $30,000 from Donald, and then Donald will be entitled to collect nothing from Drew.
choices.D: nothing from Donald, because Donald's percentage of fault is not greater than that of Pat.

(1975) 13 Cal.3d 804, 119 Cal.Rptr. 858, 532 P.2d 1226, the Supreme Court first introduced comparative negligence and eliminated the all-or-nothing doctrine of contributory negligence. Thereafter, California followed a system of pure comparative negligence, “the fundamental *358 purpose of which shall be to assign responsibility and liability for damage in direct proportion to the amount of negligence of each of the parties.” (Id. at p. 829, 119 Cal.Rptr. 858, 532 P.2d 12

3. **Common-Law Rule Pertaining to Contribution**: Under the common law, a tortfeasor who has paid more than their share of the damages can seek contribution from other tortfeasors for the excess amount paid.

Let's analyze the options:

Then, Donald could collect $40,000 from Drew, implying Drew's fault is 40%. However, this doesn't align with a scenario where Pat's recovery is limited by the abolition of joint and several liability and the specifics of comparative negligence.

- **(B)** and **(C)** Both suggest Donald is liable for $30,000, implying Donald's fault is 30%. The difference is what Donald can collect from Drew. 

- **(D)** Suggests Donald is not liable because his percentage of fault is not greater than Pat's, which doesn't directly apply to the question of how much Pat can collect from Donald if she chooses to execute against him alone.

Without explicit percentages of fault, we must infer from the options provided.

However, without explicit fault percentages, the direct calculation from the question's data is challenging. The question implies an understanding of comparative negligence principles and their application to scenarios involving multiple tortfeasors.

hyde_passage:

Therefore, if Pat executes against Donald alone, she can collect from Donald only the amount corresponding to Donald's percentage of fault, and Donald's right to contribution from other tortfeasors is limited to the amount by which his payment exceeds his proportionate share of the damages.

evidence_store.text: Comparative negligence jurisdictions allow a plaintiff to recover some percentage of his damages even though he was contributorily negligent intcausing the accident. Pure comparative negligence allows a plaintiff some recovery no matter how great his negligence was. On the MBE, examinees are instructed to assume that pure comparative negligence, with joint and several liability, is the applicable rule unless stated otherwise The doctrine of joint and several liability makes each of several tortfeasors liable to the plaintiff for the entire amount of damages to which the plaintiff is entitled. However, if one tortfeasor does have to pay the plaintiff the entire judgment, the rule of contribution allows the paying tortfeasor to recover the excess over his share from the nonpaying tortfeasor. Contribution in most comparative negligence jurisdictions is based on comparative fault, whereby the nonpaying tortfeasor is required to contribute an amount in proportion to his relative fault.

Contribution rules allow any defendant required to pay more than his share of damages under joint and several liability to have a claim against the other jointly liable parties for the excess. In states with a comparative contribution system, the traditional method of equal apportionment of damages is rejected; nonpaying tortfeasors are required to contribute only in proportion to their relative fault.

The rule of joint and several liability applies when two or more tortfeasors' actions combine to cause a plaintiff's injuries. Rozevink v. Faris, 342 N.W.2d 845, 847 (Iowa 1983). Prior to the adoption of our comparative fault act, the common-law rule required each joint tortfeasor to be jointly and severally liable for a plaintiff's entire loss. Id. at 850, modified in part by statute as stated in Johnson v. Junkmann, 395 N.W.2d 862, 867 (Iowa 1986). Our comparative fault act modified the common-law rule and provides: In actions brought under this chapter, the rule of joint and several liability shall not apply to defendants who are found to bear less than fifty percent of the total fault assigned to all parties. However, a defendant found to bear fifty percent or more of fault shall only be jointly and severally liable for economic damages and not for any noneconomic damage awards. Iowa Code § 668.4.

Most comparative negligence states have adopted a comparative contribution system based on the relative fault of the various tortfeasors. Nonpaying tortfeasors who are jointly and severally liable are required to contribute only in proportion to their relative fault.

Under a comparative negligence system, a contributorily negligent plaintiff is allowed to recover a percentage of her damages. The plaintiff's damages are reduced according to her proportionate share of the fault.

label: qa_REAL PROP._mbe_1050
subject: REAL PROP.
question: Sal owned five adjoining rectangular lots, numbered 1 through 5 inclusive, all fronting on Main Street. All of the lots are in a zone limited to one- and two-family residences under the zoning ordinance. Two years ago, Sal conveyed Lots 1, 3, and 5. None of the three deeds contained any restrictions. Each of the new owners built a one-family residence. One year ago, Sal conveyed Lot 2 to Peter. The deed provided that each of Peter and Sal, their respective heirs and assigns, would use Lots 2 and 4 respectively only for one-family residential purposes. The deed was promptly and properly recorded. Peter built a one-family residence on Lot 2. Last month, Sal conveyed Lot 4 to Betty. The deed contained no restrictions. The deed from Sal to Peter was in the title report examined by Betty's lawyer. Betty obtained a building permit and commenced construction of a two- family residence on Lot 4. Peter, joined by the owners of Lots 1, 3, and 5, brought an appropriate action against Betty to enjoin the proposed use of Lot 4, or, alternatively, damages caused by Betty's breach of covenant. Which is the most appropriate comment concerning the outcome of this action?
final_answer:
To determine the most appropriate comment concerning the outcome of this action, let's analyze the situation step by step, focusing on the key elements required for a covenant to run with the land and be enforceable against subsequent grantees.

1. **Intent for the Covenant to Run with the Land**: The deed from Sal to Peter explicitly states that both Peter and Sal, along with their heirs and assigns, would use Lots 2 and 4 for one-family residential purposes only. This indicates a clear intention for the covenant to run with the land.

2. **Privity of Estate**: For a covenant to run with the land, there must be privity of estate between the original parties and between the original party and their successors. In this case, Sal and Peter had privity of estate when the covenant was created. However, when Sal conveyed Lot 4 to Betty without any restrictions, the issue arises whether Betty is bound by the covenant due to privity of estate with Peter.

3. **Touch and Concern**: The covenant restricts the use of the land to one-family residences, which directly touches and concerns the land, as it affects the use and enjoyment of the property.

4. **Notice**: Betty's lawyer examined the title report, which included the deed from Sal to Peter containing the restriction. This implies that Betty had notice of the covenant.

Given these points, let's evaluate the options:

- **(A)** All plaintiffs being awarded an injunction due to a common development scheme is not directly relevant, as the primary issue is the enforceability of the covenant between Peter and Sal against Betty.

- **(B)** Peter should be awarded an appropriate remedy because he is a direct party to the covenant with Sal, and the covenant was intended to run with the land. The other plaintiffs (owners of Lots 1, 3, and 5) were not parties to this covenant and do not have privity of estate with Peter or Sal regarding this specific agreement. Thus, their recovery is doubtful based on the information provided.

- **(C)** Denying an injunction but awarding damages to all plaintiffs is not the most appropriate outcome, given that the primary issue is the enforceability of the covenant against Betty, and not all plaintiffs have a direct claim based on the covenant.

- **(D)** Zoning laws do not preempt private covenants in this context, especially when the covenants are more restrictive than the zoning laws.

Considering these points, the most appropriate comment is that Peter, having a direct interest in the covenant, should be awarded an appropriate remedy, but the recovery by the other plaintiffs (who were not parties to the covenant and lack the necessary privity) is doubtful.

Answer: (B)

choices.A: All plaintiffs should be awarded their requested judgment for injunction because there was a common development scheme, but award of damages should be denied to all.
choices.B: Peter should be awarded appropriate remedy, but recovery by the other plaintiffs is doubtful.
choices.C: Injunction should be denied, but damages should be awarded to all plaintiffs, measured by diminution of market value, if any, suffered as a result of the proximity of Betty's two-family residence.
choices.D: All plaintiffs should be denied any recovery or relief because the zoning preempts any private scheme of covenants.

gold_passage: It is well established that zoning ordinances cannot relieve private property from valid restrictive covenants if the ordinances are less stringent. Ridge Park Home Owners v. Pena, 88 N.M. 563, 544 P.2d 278 (1975). However, it is equally well-settled that restrictive covenants do not control a decision on the question of whether a variance should be granted by a zoning authority in a variance proceeding.
snap_answer:
To determine the most appropriate comment concerning the outcome of this action, let's analyze the situation step by step:

1. **Zoning Ordinance**: All lots are in a zone limited to one- and two-family residences. This means that, according to the zoning ordinance, Betty's plan to build a two-family residence on Lot 4 is permissible.

2. **Deed Restrictions**: The deed from Sal to Peter included a restriction that both Lots 2 and 4 would be used only for one-family residential purposes. This restriction was recorded, making it a part of the public record.

3. **Notice and Enforcement**: Since the deed from Sal to Peter was properly recorded, Betty, as a subsequent purchaser, had constructive notice of the restriction. However, for the restriction to be enforceable against Betty, it must be found in her chain of title or she must have had actual notice of it.

4. **Common Scheme**: For a covenant to run with the land, there must be a common scheme or plan for the development of the land. In this case, while there was a pattern of one-family residences being built, the absence of restrictions in the deeds for Lots 1, 3, and 5 when they were conveyed suggests that there might not have been a common scheme that included these lots from the beginning.

5. **Enforceability Against Betty**: Since the restriction was not included in the deed from Sal to Betty, and assuming there was no other notice to Betty of the restriction, the restriction may not be enforceable against her based on the covenant running with the land. However, Peter, as a party to the original agreement with Sal, has a basis for claiming that the restriction is enforceable against Sal's subsequent conveyance (to Betty), based on the terms of his deed.

6. **Damages and Injunction**: Given that Peter has a direct interest in enforcing the restriction due to his deed, he may be able to obtain an injunction or damages if Betty's actions violate the terms of the agreement between Peter and Sal. The other plaintiffs (owners of Lots 1, 3, and 5) do not have a direct basis for their claim since their deeds did not include the restriction, making their recovery doubtful.

Considering these points, the most appropriate comment concerning the outcome of this action is that Peter should be awarded an appropriate remedy because he has a direct interest in the restriction being enforced, but the recovery by the other plaintiffs is doubtful due to the lack of restrictions in their deeds.

Answer: (B)
hyde_passage: According to the doctrine of covenants running with the land, a covenant will be enforceable against subsequent purchasers of the land if it touches and concerns the land, is intended to run with the land, and if the subsequent purchaser had notice of the covenant. In this case, the covenant between Sal and Peter regarding the use of Lots 2 and 4 for one-family residential purposes may be enforceable against Betty, as a subsequent purchaser, if it is found that she had notice of the covenant, either through the public record or actual notice. However, for the other plaintiffs to enforce the covenant, they must demonstrate that they are beneficiaries of the covenant, which may be challenging given that their deeds did not include the restriction, thereby making their recovery doubtful.

evidence_store.text: A covenant at law will run with the land and be enforceable against subsequent grantees if: (i) the contracting parties intended it to run; (ii) there is privity of estate between the original promisor and promisee (horizontal privity), as well as between the promisor and his successor (vertical privity); (iii) the covenant touches and concerns the property; and (iv) the burdened party has notice of the covenant. Privity is not required to enforce an equitable servitude.

Generally, a covenant running with the land may only be enforced by the covenantee and its representatives, heirs, devisees, and assignees. See Chevy Chase Village v. Jaggers, 261 Md. 309, 314–15, 275 A.2d 167 (1971). In other words, a covenant that runs with the land may only be enforced by the covenanting parties and those in privity.

[a] covenant will "run with the land" if three conditions are met: "(1) the covenanting parties must intend to create such covenant; (2) privity of estate must exist between the person claiming [a] right to enforce the covenant and the person upon whom [the] burden of covenant is to be imposed; and (3) the covenant must 'touch and concern' the land in question."

Under Illinois law, to determine whether a covenant runs with the land, "a court looks to whether: (1) the grantee and the grantor intended the covenant to run with the land; (2) the covenant touches and concerns the land; and (3) there is privity of estate between the party claiming the benefit and the party resting under the burden of the covenant." Bank of Am., N.A. v. Cannonball LLC , 382 Ill.Dec. 562, 12 N.E.3d 841, 847-48 (2014) (citing Streams Sports Club, Ltd. v. Richmond , 99 Ill.2d 182, 75 Ill.Dec. 667, 457 N.E.2d 1226, 1230 (1983) ).

Moreover, whether the instrument is framed as a conveyance of a royalty interest or as a covenant running with the land does not impact Verde's right to recovery. A covenant will run with the land "when it [1] touches and concerns the land; [2] relates to a thing in existence or specifically binds the parties and their assigns; [3] is intended by the original parties to run with the land; and [4] when the successor to the burden has notice." Inwood N. Homeowners' Ass'n, Inc. v. Harris , 736 S.W.2d 632, 635 (Tex. 1987). Contrary to Defendants' assertions, the Mattison Deeds contemplated the conveyance of an interest in real property-a fraction of the royalty interest in the mineral estate of the Property-and thus satisfied the requirement that the covenant be memorialized in a transfer of property.

label: qa_REAL PROP._mbe_779
subject: REAL PROP.
question: Able was the owner of Blackacre, an undeveloped city lot. Able and Baker executed a written document in which Able agreed to sell Blackacre to Baker and Baker agreed to buy Blackacre from Able for $100,000; the document did not provide for an earnest money down payment. Able recorded the document, as authorized by statute. Able orally gave Baker permission to park his car on Blackacre without charge prior to the closing. Thereafter, Baker frequently parked his car on Blackacre. Another property came on the market that Baker wanted more than Blackacre. Baker decided to try to escape any obligation to Able. Baker had been told that contracts for the purchase and sale of real property require consideration and concluded that because he had made no earnest money down payment, he could refuse to close and not be liable. Baker notified Able of his intention not to close and, in fact, did refuse to close on the date set for the closing. Able brought an appropriate action to compel specific performance by Baker. If Able wins, it will be because
final_answer:
To solve this question, let's analyze the information provided and the options given.

First, let's consider the nature of the contract between Able and Baker. They have a written document outlining the terms of the sale of Blackacre, which includes the price and the intention to transfer ownership. This document, although not further detailed, implies a contractual agreement between the two parties.

Option (A) suggests that Baker's use of Blackacre for parking constitutes part performance. In contract law, part performance can make a contract enforceable even if it lacks consideration, especially in cases involving real estate. However, Baker's use of the property with Able's permission does not necessarily constitute part performance of the contract, as it was done with permission and not as a direct result of the contractual obligations.

Option (B) mentions that general contract rules regarding consideration apply to real estate contracts. In contract law, consideration is a crucial element for a contract to be enforceable. However, the absence of an earnest money down payment does not negate the existence of consideration, as the promise to buy and sell can itself be consideration.

Option (C) refers to the doctrine of equitable conversion. This doctrine applies when there is a valid contract for the sale of land, making the purchaser the equitable owner of the property from the time the contract is signed. This doctrine is relevant in determining the risk of loss and the rights of the parties before the actual transfer of the deed. However, the question revolves around the enforceability of the contract rather than the risk of loss or the equitable ownership.

Option (D) states that the document was recorded. The recording of a document can provide notice to others about the transaction and can be relevant in priority disputes, but it does not directly impact the enforceability of the contract between the parties.

Given the context of the question, which is about compelling specific performance, the key issue is whether the contract between Able and Baker is enforceable. Specific performance is an equitable remedy that can be granted when a party has failed to perform their contractual obligations, particularly in cases involving real estate where monetary damages may not be adequate.

The correct answer hinges on understanding that the contract between Able and Baker, despite Baker's claim about lack of consideration due to no earnest money down payment, is enforceable. The doctrine of equitable conversion (Option C) is relevant here because it implies that once a valid contract for the sale of land is entered into, the purchaser (Baker) becomes the equitable owner of the property. This doctrine supports the enforceability of the contract, making it possible for Able to seek specific performance.

Therefore, the reason Able wins is because of the principles related to the enforceability of real estate contracts and the equitable considerations that support specific performance.

Answer: (C)

choices.A: Baker's use of Blackacre for parking constitutes part performance.
choices.B: general contract rules regarding consideration apply to real estate contracts.
choices.C: the doctrine of equitable conversion applies.
choices.D: the document was recorded.
